Text: So knife crime's going to go up, because the evidence is there, Carla. Reduce stop-and-search, knife crime soars. No, it doesn't.

Of course Gordon Brown's right to say there's a link. Michael, you know this better than we do. You know there's a link between poverty at home and underperformance in the classroom. It's that link which is holding back so many children. That's what's unfair. That link is the link that I want to help solve. We would do it partly through the tax proposals I've talked about earlier, giving people £700 back in their pocket by raising the income tax threshold to £10,000 so that people on ordinary incomes who aren't being helped at the moment are helped. And through our proposal, we call it - it's a slightly technocratic phrase - we call it a pupil premium. It basically means extra money, £2.5 billion. That would, for instance, allow our schools to reduce the average class size in an average primary school down to 20. I have three young children. Two of them go to a local school, at eight and five. I see myself as a father that what happens to a young child in a reception class, years one, two and three, is so important in developing their self-confidence, their social skills, their willingness to learn. Get it right at that early age, and we can really help people later in life. That's what I believe in.
You won't admit the truth, will you? The truth is there's nothing we can do.

I tell you how we pay for it. We would, for instance, stop the huge unfair loopholes that only benefit the very wealthy at the top of the tax system. At the moment, the top 10% earners in this country get twice as much tax subsidy from all of the rest of you when they make contributions to their pension pot than everybody else. We say give people tax relief on their pension contributions, but make sure that they are the same and use that money so that no-one pays any income tax on the first £10,000 they earn.
You just, look, there's no point in speculating about that at the moment, cos we're a long way off taking the decision.

I'm just talking numbers. We haven't even mentioned housing yet. Oh, no, I ignore that nonsense. Complete nonsense. I'm just talking pure facts. Housing. We have to build one new home every two minutes just to cope with legal net migration under this Conservative government. It is literally impossible. And as for the numbers coming in, I would respond to this. 50% of the three quarters of a million who are coming in a year are dependents. They're not wealth creators. And mass migration is making us poorer. You'll hear, oh, GDP's going up. Well, of course it is. There are more people. GDP per capita, our relative wealth, has declined for the last six consecutive quarters.

We need strong big business in this country. We do, and one thing, can I just come in on that David? Yes, yes. But one thing I think business needs, and again this is a difference of view, I think we need to stay within the European Union. Because I think it would be a disaster for our businesses. I mean, give David Cameron credit, he believes in us staying in the EU, but I'm afraid he's being dragged by his party to exit from the EU. Now again, I've got to say to this audience, I've got to say to this audience, I think that would be a real problem for our country.

have to spend money wisely. Folks, I've explained to you, we've massively increased over the last few years the percentage of the national cake we put into the NHS for a worse return. Does nobody else acknowledge we need to spend the money better?

Let me perhaps draw out one of them. David Cameron keeps repeating we've got nothing to say. We're the only party, actually, in this general election campaign who've got a plan fully costed to get people off benefits and into work. That's been independently recognised. I think it's a general... It's almost a philosophical issue here. Do you think the best way to help people who are vulnerable or poor is to constantly giving them more benefits through greater dependency on the state? Or do you provide incentives to let them help themselves where they can? That is what our very, very radical tax switch, so you keep more money when you start working, would precisely do - it would get people off that benefits dependency into work. I think that's what most people agree is the absolute priority these days.

Well, we won't be able to do anything unless we win the election. We've obviously only been in opposition so far. I have been frustrated, if I'm honest, that these strikes have gone on as long as they have gone on. The strikes are very bad for patients. People are very anxious about the strikes. I don't think doctors want to be on strike. As I said earlier, my wife works in one of the big London hospitals, so I know first-hand and relayed every night, pressure that NHS staff are under. It's been unrelenting during Covid, unrelenting now because of waiting lists. So what we would do is instead of arguing about who gets in the room first to negotiate with the doctors, we would roll up our sleeves in a grown-up way, get in the room and settle this dispute so that patients can get the care they need. Doctors can get back to work, and the country can move forward, because we can't go on like this anymore. And the sooner we have a bit of grown-up politics in this, the better, I think, because we absolutely need to move forward as a country.

So Scotland has a migration problem. The problem is we don't have enough migrants because Scotland has a declining working age population. So we need people to come. And you know what? I am genuinely fed up going to speak to businesses. going to speak to health care workers, going to speak to people in our social care sector, in our schools and beyond, who say that they need more colleagues. Businesses who want to expand, who need people to come and work with them and for them. That's why I'm in favour of migration. And this race to the right that is happening in Westminster is so, so wrong. We can do better. We must demand better, not just from the Tories, but from the Labour Party too. And the SNP will always do that.
The reality is the SNP can't do just the same as Tory does. On the point of the anti-austerity motion that Ed Miliband talks about...
I believe in making a success of Brexit.

Thank you, Julie, and everyone watching tonight. Firstly, I want to express my condolences to everyone affected by Friday's attack. This atrocity should give us all pause for thought. None of us yet know the full details, so politicians should avoid knee-jerk responses. But we do know that our justice system has been hollowed out. Violent crime is up. Our prison's in crisis. Our police overstretched. Listen to nurses and they'll say the same about our National Health Service. Teachers the same about our schools. Deep cuts to vital public services. The fabric of our communities. Happened at the same time the government made huge tax giveaways to the richest few and to big business. Labour will make very different choices. We will be on your side, rebuilding our country for the many, not the few.

What happened was completely wrong and the Prime Minister has rightly apologised for that, apologised to veterans but also to all of us because he was representing all of us. I'm from Portsmouth, I've also been Defence Secretary and my wish at the end of this week is that all of our veterans feel completely treasured. And I'm hoping tonight to convince you of some things that are important to them, important to their legacy. And I couldn't do that if I wasn't straight with you on that issue.

I would like to see a levelling upwards when it comes to fairness, because what I'm deeply worried about is the kinds of rules, for example, that will break up families, that will end up in things like immigration detention, that has things like a minimum income rule. I think all of those are deeply unfair. Family reunion ought to be supported, so we need fair immigration rules.

No, I voted for remain. I wanted to remain, but now we've left and there's no going back. But... Sorry? No, I'm not. No, that is not our policy. Never has been our policy. I voted for remain, but we voted to leave. We're out. I accept that. We're not going back. Do you not see here... But we do need... We've got a very botched deal. If you talk to any business that's trying to trade, they will tell you it's not good enough. I think we can do more on defence and security, by the way, with our European partners. But we're not going back in. We're not going back into the single market, the customs union, and we're not going back to freedom of movement. That is very, very clear and set out in our manifesto, because we did have a referendum, it split us down the middle, and we do not want to go through that again. But you can see why.
Tell me how you're going to balance the books.

But you said that you had a point about the policy. I'm just telling you, I disagree with you. 15 other European countries just a few weeks ago decided that they agree with me. Did the Supreme Court call it unlawful? Well, I've been crystal clear, and that's why we've passed a new piece of legislation to address the concerns of our court, because I respect our court. They raised concerns, that's why we passed a new piece of legislation, and that's why their flights will now go if I'm Prime Minister. And when it comes to the ECHR, I am crystal clear. I believe everything that we are doing is in compliance with our international obligations, but if a foreign court, including the European Court, forces me to choose between our country's national security and membership of that court, I'm going to choose this country's national security every single time.
And there'd be more refugees then, yeah?
Greens holding you to account on that in the Parliament. Doing a good job.
You voted for the Bedroom Tax nine times, are you proud about that as well?

No, no, no. What I do think we need to do is we've got to provide more council housing and more social housing, but to restore people's trust, I think it has to be done by local councils and it must be for local people.

I want to thank you for watching tonight. A vote for Labour is a vote for hope. We are the party that created our NHS, introduced the minimum wage and will end NHS privatisation, introduce a living wage of at least £10 an hour and end the scandalous crisis in social care that leaves 1.5 million older people without the care they need. A Labour government will support you and invest in your community. We are on your side. Let's bring real change to our country so that it works for the many, not the few. Vote Labour on the 12th of December.

You've just admitted, if the population's growing by 2.5 million people in the last three years alone, you haven't got a labour shortage. You've got to make work pay, you've got to train your own people. We haven't got a people shortage. There's no labour shortage. Does anyone else agree there's no labour shortage? We haven't got a people shortage, but too many people, work doesn't pay.

Plaid Cymru want to build 10,000 new affordable houses over the next 10 years. But we also want to make owning and running a household more affordable. So we want to create a Welsh energy company, Ynni Cymru, that will allow us to use Welsh natural resources to bring down the cost of energy for families and houses across Wales. We want to scrap the cruel and unnecessary bedroom tax. And we want to scrap letting agency fees so that it's cheaper to rent.

But, Nick, The Institute of Fiscal Studies that you were quoting a few minutes ago says that your proposals for financing your tax cuts are highly speculative, they don't know how you'e going to get the money. That's what they say about your proposals. They're not thought out, you're too big a risk on the economy, just like David is. We are trying to get people back to work in difficult situations, low inflation, low interest rates, get the economy moving, don't take money and shrink the economy now. That's the way to get people back to work.

Well, the truth is that trust has absolutely collapsed in politicians, because people are sick and tired of politicians promising things and then not delivering. And we saw that with Labour Party manifestos. We saw it with the referendum, of course, when the £9 million Conservative government leaflet promised to carry out the will of the people, and it's not yet been done. We have to get it done, and after that, trust can start to be rebuilt.

Well, Carly, politics is about choices. So inevitably, people will have differing views. And the electors decide who they want to carry out that policy for the next four or five years. But I did in a previous question talk about the National Health Service. There are areas where it seems everybody wants the same thing. And when it comes to resolving the care crisis, when it comes to the National Health Service being free at the point of delivery, there is actually quite broad agreement across all the parties. So there should be areas in which parties can work together. Whilst everybody here agrees on climate change, I've got a slight issue with that, in that what we're doing is putting a lot of people out of work and making the poor give lots of money to the rich. Yes, we want a cleaner planet, but I don't think the way we're currently doing it is right. So there'll always be disagreement.

Well it's possibly the question that is uppermost in everybody's mind right now and of course all of our thoughts remain with those affected by that horrific and cowardly attack in the centre of London on Saturday night and of course with the people of Manchester as well. This is one of the greatest responsibilities for any politician. and I think the first thing politicians need to do is be honest. There are no easy answers and often the knee-jerk responses are the wrong ones. Firstly, we've got to tackle and address and challenge extremism wherever we find it. And I believe very strongly that we have to do that with the Muslim community. We mustn't scapegoat the Muslim community, because it's wrong. Most Muslims are as appalled at these attacks as the rest of us. But it would also be counterproductive. because we're going to find it easier to root out extremism if we're working in partnership with that community. Secondly, we've got to make sure we invest in our security and intelligence services, and we've got to make sure we invest in our police. Obviously, one of the big issues of debate today has been the reduction in police numbers in England, 20,000 fewer police officers. In Scotland, we've maintained police numbers and we've increased the numbers of police officers who are trained to bear firearms. So after the Manchester attack, Police Scotland was able to have an increased level of policing from within its own resources without calling on the military, although we're very grateful for the offer of help from the military. So these are the things we've got to do. Two final points I would make are these. I've made one of them already about not scapegoating particular communities. But secondly, we must make sure that in our determination, and all of us share it to keep the population safe, we don't start to undermine our own freedoms and civil liberties, because these are part of what makes us who we are.

Well, absolutely right. The gentleman has got it nailed on the head. I mean, north of England is the most disenfranchised part of the United Kingdom. Look, I want Scotland to be independent. I'm not here to advocate for Yorkshire independence. I'll leave you to do that if you wish. But look, I absolutely see the benefit that Scotland has had, even with the limited powers of devolution that we have. So yes, of course, the people of northern England, I think, have been the most disenfranchised. I just want to touch upon one or two things that were said by Katrina, and I think the question was exceptionally important. We're believers in a proportional system. In Scotland we have a hybrid proportional system which works well in the Scottish Parliament. It's designed not to have a majority. There's only been one majority in the history of devolution and that's because we literally broke the system. But it's never been, it's not designed for a majority and it forces the SNP, who's the government at the moment, to work with other parties to find a compromise. So we definitely would support that. Also support, I should say, the abolition of the House of Lords. We've never taken a peerage, never ever will.

Charlotte, you're right. We've got to live within our means as a country. And that's why under my plan, we'll cut the deficit every year and we'll balance the books. But we'll do it a different way from David Cameron. And I reject his plan to double the spending cuts next year, because I think for you and so many people in this audience who rely on our NHS, that would be a disaster. So here's our plan. Our plan is to have fair taxes, to reverse David Cameron's tax cut for millionaires, which has given every millionaire in Britain £43,000 a year. It's to clamp down on that tax avoidance and raise seven and a half billion pounds a year. It's to protect education and health. But outside some key areas like that, to see spending fall. And it's also about something else. Because by raising living standards of working people in this country, we can get more tax revenues in, and we can get the deficit down. It's a fair plan. It's a better plan. A better plan from David Cameron's carrying on as he is.
Yes.

Look, what I'm being really clear to people about is that I want to make sure we maximise the Liberal Democrat number of MPs. We're basically going to beat lots of Conservative MPs, I hope, a few in Scotland, a few SNP MPs, but I'm determined to focus in on that. I've been in politics... OK, so that's not a question you're going to answer, just to be clear. Well, I'll explain why, Fiona. I've been in politics a little while. I've seen a lot of Lib Dem leaders. And sometimes they focus on what happens after the election. And I've noticed that when they do that, they don't focus on the job in hand, which is trying to win local champions and Liberal Democrats across the country. And I'm going to try and do that.

And we have, not they do have problems, but what they don't have are the alternative systems for people to make use of. All the indications show that the public will use public transport, if it's reliable, if it's affordable, and if it's safe. There wasn't adequate alternative methods of transportation in both those cities at the time. The ground work could not be done. Contrast and compare.

No, and look, his sums don't add up. But what I would say to Charlotte and the audience here, and indeed at home, is you've got to make a judgment about two things in this debate, balance and fairness. Look, I think there's some people on the stage who'll say there don't have to be any cuts at all. There'll be some people like Nigel who wants to follow David Cameron's extreme plan to double the cuts next year, which I think will be a disaster for our public services. And then this question of fairness. Look, Nigel actually wants to cut the top rate of income tax even further for the highest earners in our country, another £43,000 tax cut. Now, I just have a different view about the way a country succeeds. I think this idea that you just help the rich and powerful and the wealth will trickle down to everyone else is not the answer. You put working people first. So think about these two things as you cast your vote and make your decision. Balance and fairness. I believe ours is a balanced plan and a fair plan.
Yes, inflation is... Of course, of course, I know things are not... I know things are not easy. Of course I know that. They've been difficult for a while for all sorts of reasons.
The first thing we've got to do is...

So imagine your mum has a fall, and then imagine those one in four people in our National Health Service who are from overseas aren't there anymore. And you remember that the people who are working in this country from other countries are hard-working, caring and decent, just like the rest of us. Are there pressures caused by immigration? Yes, there are. Should they be tackled by politicians providing the infrastructure? Yes, of course, they must be. But this is about what kind of a country we are. Setting false low targets, as Theresa May has done, as Paul Nuttall endorses, doesn't put Britain first. It's about appeasing the farages of this world rather than doing what is right by British business, by British health service, by British farmers, by the British economy.
My advice to you, Caroline, and certainly to Jeremy Corbyn if he was here, is don't make promises. Don't make promises you can't keep. And the reason I kept my promise...

I'm afraid to say I think the responsibility for a British government at this stage, two years after the event, with the loss of life we've suffered, with the amount of innocent civilians in Iraq, that we have been...

No, no, no. I'll give you the example of this. I'm a very keen footballer. I still play at my age. And a few years ago, I damaged my knee. My meniscus got torn. Really, really painful. Stopped me playing. I went to the NHS. They put me on a waiting list like everybody else. And I waited my turn and had it fixed. And I'm back playing football. I'm very grateful for that. I do just... So I didn't say if one of my children was going to die. What I would say about acute care, because, I mean, you all know this, If you're dealing with acute care, my mum was very, very ill all of her life. I spent a lot of my teenage years in high dependency units with my mum on nights we thought we were going to lose her because she was so ill. It absolutely seared on me what the NHS did for my mum. I know, and everybody knows, that for acute cases, life-threatening cases, the NHS is the very best place to be. So much so that private hospitals, in fact, refer into the NHS at that point because the NHS has got the expertise and the facility. So no, I wouldn't say to the rest of the population, you can be on a waiting list, give me time to fix it, but I'm going to speed myself through. I'm not going to look people in the eye and say, that's not me. If a family member was in an acute, life-threatening situation, I would want them in the NHS. because I know, from personal experience, the NHS is absolutely the best place to be if there's a life-threatening situation. That is my genuine view on this.
I think the honest answer is that we've had a very tough time as a party in the last couple of years. And as a consequence of all of those difficulties, I'm here to lead the Scottish National Party.

Well, Shirley, I'm really grateful for your service in the NHS too and I suspect, like you, my approach to the NHS is personal rather than political. I think about my mum's own journey through ovarian cancer, from the diagnosis to the treatment. to the care, to her passing away on a hospital ward just two floors away from the one where she'd given birth to me all those years previously. I think of the young woman in Kendal who came to see me last week, 19 years of age, eating disorder, told that she could not get treatment because she was not yet sick enough. Are the people who work in our NHS the best in the world? Absolutely. Is our NHS the best funded in the world? Don't kid yourself. It is time politicians were honest with the British people that there needs to be more money and that if there's going to be more money we will all have to pay for it. That is why the Liberal Democrats will put one penny on income tax to raise £30 billion over a parliament to invest in our national health and social care service so you get the treatment you deserve from cradle to grave.
No, they weren't wrong in what they reported at the time, they were absolutely right. But later it transpired that
Well, I would love to do that job. But if it does happen, then the democratic mandate is there.

I'm looking at Janet's response. When all the Prime Minister can say to her is that what the position is in Wales, Janet, I don't know. You may not be interested in the position in Wales. You may be interested in the position that you're facing and want a Prime Minister who takes responsibility for what he is responsible for. But what you'll get with the Prime Minister is somebody who makes a promise saying, I will be held personally responsible for bringing those waiting lists down. At the moment, they don't come down. What does he do? He blames everybody else. It's a personal responsibility, actually.
We can say sorry all you want. See if these guys made a mistake nine times in their career.

Well, of course, you're predicting the future that Scotland's going to be independent. And I very much hope that's the case. And there's very many Eastern European nations which have joined the European Union whilst having a debt ratio and a deficit ratio, sorry, which is higher than that which is currently in place, as long as they have a plan in place to bring it down. And we would most certainly have a plan in place to bring it down. But we are the most pro-European party in Parliament, and we will stand true to our values.

What happened? Well, we abolished the fees. You don't pay for tuition in Scotland. And what happens now? And what happens now is that the rate of application from Scots... Hang on, hang on. What happens financially now?
Just don't call us a loony party because that's offensive language actually.

Yes. I didn't see it. But I had the Attorney General's advice, and the Attorney General, made it absolutely clear that provided that we could show that there were breaches of the United Nations resolutions.

Well it was important to me that given the seriousness and the sensitivity of the matters at hand, that they were dealt with properly. And that's what I've done. We had an independent internal inquiry into what happened, have taken action and suspended the candidates involved, and I've been crystal clear that they should face the full consequences of the law. But Keir Starmer talks about leadership in politics. That's what Sue's question was about. It was a broad question about how to do that. I also think leadership means being straight with people. It means having the courage of your convictions, not changing your mind on every single position that you've taken, and being upfront about what you want to do for the country. That's not what Keir Starmer is doing. He's not being honest with everyone about his plans to raise their taxes. I think that will erode trust in politics. And that's the choice at this election.
No, you wanted a programme, Olsen, within two weeks where we couldn't debate the motion, which is not usual process.

vote SNP MPs in and we will support any party that looks to abolish the House of Lords. You may not have all your voters here. I promise you that. Just finish that one point please. Let me just make one point, it's exceptionally important that voting is absolutely one part, one way that you can take part in the democratic process. Can I say I'm really proud in the party to have somebody like Mary Black, the youngest ever MP in the House of Commons. Don't just use your vote, get involved, get yourself elected and sit down in Westminster because you can make a difference too.

We have been speaking to experts, many experts, who've proposed a wide range of different ways in which you can ask the very richest in society, people with wealth of 10 million or more, to pay modestly more. We're talking about 1% on assets over 10 million. But if you did that, you could raise tens of billions by the end of this next parliament.

Yeah, but sometimes, Anoushka, some of the questions are a bit more complicated than yes and no. I think we all know that. So you would if he was president? Listen, America is one of our closest allies, and you have to respect whoever they elect.
This is a UK government and a UK government policy.
I haven't seen his numbers. I can tell you my numbers. But I can give you a...

Gordon Brown is trying to make you believe he can protect health spending, he can protect education spending, he can protect police spending. He cannot do any of these things because he's given this country the biggest budget deficit of any developed country in the world. I'm going to stop it there.

There are no plans whatsoever to disband MI5 or any other part of the security services. There are plans to invest properly in our police and security services. And when you live and represent an area like mine, where somebody was murdered on the streets by a far-right terrorist attack on an individual, you realise how horrific that is for the community and how dangerous that is for all of us. Yes, we do need to invest in all of our services, and that will help to make us all a lot safer.
And here's our NHS, is what he would say. Oh, nonsense.

Nobody can guarantee the real-terms per-pupil funding increase. In the Labour Party's manifesto we know the figures don't add up. What is important is that as we look What we need to do is to ensure we will put those record levels of funding into schools. We need to ensure that we get that better spread of funding in terms of the fair funding formula. But I also say this, if you look at what happens in education, it's not just people are focusing on funding, but actually we need to ensure that we do see good or outstanding schools and more good or outstanding places in good or outstanding schools for children. We've seen the number increase over the last seven years. We need to carry on doing that so that there is a good school place for every child. And that isn't just about funding, it's about a whole variety of things that go to make up really good education for children.

Well, of course, in Scotland, it's the SNP that has seen the building as oversaw the building of 40% more affordable homes than in England and 70% more affordable homes in Wales. I don't necessarily think that's a tough question to answer. What we are looking at is the future and ultimately the Conservatives. have decided to cut the capital budget for Scotland, which impacts directly upon our ability to build more homes. But for the people who are living within those homes, what we've done in Scotland is introduced the Scottish Child Payment. It doesn't exist anywhere else on these isles. The Labour Party won't replicate it, the Tories certainly won't. 100,000 children are going to be lifted out of poverty thanks to that Scottish Child Payment. It is being called a game-changer. Will the Labour Party back it? Yes or no?

Sure. Alicia. Alicia. Alicia. It's about leadership, Alicia. It's about leadership and what I want to achieve as Prime Minister. When I look at the country, what do I think the biggest problems in the country are? I think five years of wages falling behind bills and the threat it's going to happen again. I think five years of young people thinking they're going to have a worse life than their parents. And five years of an NHS in crisis. And if I'm Prime Minister, in seven or eight days' time, I want to spend all of my energies on those issues, not on deciding whether we want to exit the European Union, which I think, frankly, would be a disaster for the country and would set us back. But supposing she wants a voice vote?

All I would say is this argument, I think, just doesn't address the fundamental issue. There are going to be big things over the next few years, and neither of them will come clean on this with you, that we simply can't afford to do. Trident. I don't think we can afford it. A tax on banks I think is now unavoidable. Tax credits. We need to look at public sector pensions. These are big decisions which we need to take. And I would like us for once to get politicians together.

I think it's absolutely vital. I think it's absolutely vital, and I think that the issue of trust in politics is central to this election and fundamental to the corrosion of trust in politics at the moment. So why do you think you're being asked that question? Let's be clear. It's the failure of politicians to deliver Brexit. Because the people of the EU... Hang on. Hang on a minute.

Well, I'm going to try and change... Let me see if I can change your mind. Look, look, I think that the thing that's happening in Britain at the moment is it's the very richest in our society. And I'm talking about the top 1% people earning over £150,000 a year who are doing sort of OK. And I think lots and lots of people, including on middle incomes, are finding life really tough. Now, the question is how we do something about it. And I hope you'll get a different message from me tonight if that's the message you've been getting from me. Let me give you an example. Tuition fees. Some people have criticised me for wanting to lower the burden of tuition fees from £9,000 to £6,000 because they say it'll help middle-class families. Well, too right I want to help middle-class families. Because I think lots of families are saying, if my son or daughter is leaving university with £44,000 of debt, that isn't a good situation, let's do something about it with fair changes. So, look, I want a country where you feel you're not struggling, you can get on, and that's one of the changes we'd make.

No, no, if you look at the sum total of the time that I've been Prime Minister, because you've picked one particular period, if you look at the sum total of the time that I've been Prime Minister, the numbers are down.

So the way to deal with this is to make sure that there are safe and legal routes set up where necessary and I've mentioned some of the examples before like the Afghan scheme and the Syrian scheme. What we can't have is people illegally entering the country by small boat From a safe place, France is a safe country, nobody has to leave France to flee danger. The people coming across on small boats are overwhelmingly young, single men. We should be prioritising people who are in genuine need. The people that came from Syria, for example, very often they were women and children and people who needed support. So we cannot have this small boat traffic continuing. And that is why the Rwanda scheme is so important. It acts as a deterrent. Richard, in case you haven't noticed, the Rwanda scheme has actually not started yet. Well, it's a very good question, so there's a couple of... The Home Office hasn't... Well, I'll give you the answer. I'll give you the answer. So, the Australians tried something similar about ten years ago. They called it Operation Sovereign Borders. They completely stopped what they called their illegal maritime arrivals. That's because they had a pick-up policy, they had a push-back policy. Using removals to, in that case, Nauru. But secondly, we set up a returns agreement with Albania about a year and a half ago, and that saw... A 94% reduction in Albanians crossing the English Channel because they knew if they came across there'd be a return. So there are two very clear case studies showing you how a return scheme or removal scheme acts as a deterrent. It'll end these dangerous and illegal and unnecessary crossings, giving us capacity to focus our attention on those people who are genuinely in need.

Yeah, I mean, I'm going to just be frank on this, and it may not always be popular. Firstly, I don't believe EU nationals should lose rights that they've got. I think the way in which EU nationals who've made a massive contribution to this country have been treated since Brexit has been absolutely shameful. But I do think we should make it more possible to attract talent from non-EU countries. As you know, I'm the First Minister of Scotland. If you look ahead for 25 years, all of the growth in Scotland's population is projected to come from inward migration. If we make it harder, then the risk is our population will fall and that will have a negative impact on our economy. I also hate the tone of the debate around immigration, the hostile environment demonising immigrants. Imagine if British citizens living in other countries were talked about and treated in the way that immigrants here are sometimes talked about. It's shameful, it's wrong and we need to change it for hard economic reasons as well as for bigger value-based reasons.

I think it's also important to recognise the biodiversity crisis in our oceans as well and off our own coasts. We would want to ban fossil fuel licences for exploration, for instance in Cardigan Bay, and also see the UK signing the UN Global Ocean Treaty, which seeks to create a network of ocean sanctuaries across the whole of the Earth, which will be worth 30% of our oceans.

Well I want the parties to work together and when I became Prime Minister I invited one or two liberals to do things like Shirley Williams to do things for the government. I invited people who are businessmen and women to come into the government and work with us for national economic benefit and I will continue to ask people to give their talents to public service and that is something that's really important to do. But there is this fundamental position about this year and I think we've got to resolve this. I've had to take the economy through the most difficult global financial crisis. We made the right decisions to take ownership of the banks and the right decisions to get the world together to deal with that recession. Every time we've said we have to support the economy through difficult times. Now, what David's saying is even when it's fragile, he wants for ideological reasons, I think, to take six billion pounds out of the economy. That puts thousands of jobs, teachers, It puts policemen, it puts thousands of jobs at risk immediately. And that's why they're talking about an emergency budget in June. David, you're a risk to the economy. Nick's a risk to our security with his nuclear weapons policy. You're a risk to the economy.

Exactly right. Let's think about one group of people. There are 800,000 people over the age of 65 in Britain at the moment. who need social care who aren't getting it. Those are people who need help getting out of bed in the morning, people who need help to feed themselves, need help bathing, need help to live a basically decent civilised life. They're not getting that help. And what the Green Party is saying, we have to provide that help and at the same time ensure that the NHS is providing the services we need. We want to create 400,000 new jobs in social care in the NHS. Those are jobs, I don't know what your plans in life are, Charlotte, but perhaps one of those jobs might be yours. And those are jobs that ensure that the most vulnerable in our society are being looked after, are being cared for. And of course, the people who get those jobs will also be paying tax and national insurance and have some money to spend in their local communities.
There's a cap on the welfare bill which the government has put in place, we're going to keep to within that cap.

No, I don't have a particular figure in mind. We do say that it would be lower, it would be 40% under a Conservative government after a period of time as compared to 42% under Labour. and you've always got to balance the need for proper public services with the need for a low-tax economy which will encourage people to use their hard work, their skills, their energy, their enterprise to the full. And it's a balanced judgment that you have to make at each budget every year. Okay.

I've been talking with Nigel about our plans for months and we've been doing well but we want to put turbo boosters, rocket boosters onto our campaign and that's what we've done. I'm delighted that Nigel's agreed both to stand, to lead the party and off we go. We're a successful team. In 2019 we won a national election with Mia's chair, with Nigel a leader. I'm delighted that Nigel's getting stuck... Not at all. Look, we had 1,000 people in a constituency where Nigel's standing today in Clacton for his campaign launch. We're off.

I don't know about you, but I thought all of that sounded slightly desperate. It sounded like an attempt to frighten people, instead of doing what I think we need to do in our country, which is to take and make a clean break from the last 13 years, to have new leadership to take the country forward, to solve the problems that we have. And I think that the Conservatives are best placed to offer that, because if you vote Conservative, you know you can get fresh, new leadership from a new team on May the 7th, rather than being stuck with what we have now. You've heard in the debate today some big differences set out between us about how we believe you've got to give more priority to keeping our country safe, keeping our borders secure, making sure we keep our defences strong. But also you've heard a lot of differences on values, how the family comes first for me, how we need to do more to help those who actually do the right thing and want their government behind them. We do face some difficult times in this country, but I still think we have incredible days ahead of us. We're a great country in so many different ways, with the right leadership, with the right values, with a clean break from these 13 years of failure. We can achieve anything if we all pull together and build the big society that we all know we need to make our country a better place to live in.

And by doing help to buy and abolishing stamp duty, we will massively be able to speed up how quickly you can get on the housing ladder and fulfill that dream of owning your own home. I think it's incredibly important. And as I said, I don't want to give you waffle, two very specific things, help to buy, abolish stamp duty, neither backed by Keir Starmer and the Labour Party. So again, there's a very clear choice for you. Because if he's your prime minister, stamp duty for first-time buyers is going up. Right, that's what the plans are. If he's your Prime Minister, stamp duty...
Charles Kennedy.

These two constantly argue about waste, as if we can fill the black hole in the public finances by saving money on paper clips and pot plants in Whitehall. Of course we can get rid of a bit of waste, but that doesn't really address the big questions we need to ask ourselves. And I think we need to be clear with you, open with you, straight with you, We've tried to do that. We set out £15 billion worth of savings. I've listed some of them. We have one specific tax that we want to introduce to help fill the black hole. We would impose a 10% tax on the profits of the banks. These banks who got us into the trouble in the first place, I think they should pay you back because you, the taxpayer, have bailed them out and use that money to deal with the black hole in the finances. But let's not get obsessed about mythical savings in waste. which is the oldest trick in the book to pretend that you can square a circle like that, or get obsessed about when you deliver these cuts. The crucial thing is, are we going to be open with people, with you, about how we're going to save money in the long term?

Thank you. Let me bring in... I think this goes to the heart of the problem, because I think we should just keep political dogma out of the public sector and public services. The public... We're all politicians up here, but most of the public don't care what colour the cat is. They just want some mice caught. That's what they want. They want results. And in my experience, listening to people who are actually doing these jobs, police officers who have reduced crime by half, over the time we've been in office. Healthcare professionals who are coming up with amazing initiatives in our hospitals and in our GP surgeries. Teachers who are responsible. We now have 90% of our schools are good or outstanding. Listen to the professionals in these services. Keep political dogma out of it.

Countries that have brought in a wealth tax, like, for example, Switzerland and Spain, have done so very effectively. And it makes a big difference if we're going to put in this investment that we need. We're the only party that's being honest in this election.

What happened in Manchester was unbelievably abominable in every way. Innocent lives were taken of mainly young women out enjoying themselves. I want to live in a country that protects the right of people to go out and enjoy themselves at any town or any city in this country. The consequences of that are appalling for those families, and I hope those that are perpetrated this act are all discovered and brought to book. But there's also a question we have to ask about the number of police officers. Under Amber Rudd's time in the Home Secretary and Theresa May before that, 20,000 police officers have lost their jobs. There are fewer police officers around than there were seven years ago, and those cuts are going to continue. I think it's important that we restore that. What I also think, if I may say so, is the point that Angus was alluding to about terror threats and other threats to our security. There are terrorist threats, obviously. There are also cyber attacks threats, as the National Health Service discovered. It's also important that we recognize that leaving large spaces of, for example, Libya, ungoverned, leaves an opportunity for those who wish to do harm to an awful lot of people, a space in which to develop to do that harm. If you intervene somewhere, the consequences go on for a very long time.

The answer is to have a competent asylum process that processes people quickly and promptly with a rapid appeals process so that actually the people who are genuine asylum seekers can be accepted and those who are not, who are economic migrants, can be rejected. But the truth is that the Conservative Party have allowed mass immigration in the last two years deliberately in complete contrast to what they promised election after election after election. One person is arriving legally every minute. That means we need to build a new home just for those arriving every two minutes. How many leave? How often do they leave? That's the net number. How many do they leave? The net number in 2023 was almost 700,000 people, just below the net number in 2022. So those numbers I've just given you, that is the net increase. We're not building enough homes for our own people, let alone the homes for people... No, we're not.

Well, look, this might be an immigration election, but it is definitely going to be a cost-of-living election. And one thing that we can do to help the gentleman who asked the question is about ensuring that you keep more of your money. We will cut your taxes. Labour will put your taxes up. Was that the question? If you're having more agency, being able to get onto the property ladder, that is the key in this general election.

The problem is... Hear me out, Harry, this is really important. Tens of thousands of people are here not processed. They're here for life. That's not a deterrent. If it was a deterrent, the numbers wouldn't be going up. They're going up.

So Lorenzo, your question started by saying, talking about houses, roads and GPs. We need to build 150,000 social homes a year to tackle the crisis in our housing. The roads are in a mess because the Conservatives have cut local authority budgets between 25 and 40%. That's why public services are on their knees. and the Conservatives promised at the last general election to recruit 6,000 GPs. We now have fewer GPs per head right across our country. That's why our public services are on their knees.

This is terrible leadership on the part of Donald Trump. It's also appalling leadership. on behalf of the Prime Minister, who can't even be bothered to come here and answer these questions for herself today. But it's no surprise either. He was very clear during his election campaign what it was he would be about in terms of climate change, and he is sticking to his line. But he is wrong, and the world needs to tell him he is wrong. It's bad business. It's bad, bad business.

When somebody is diagnosed with a dreadful illness, my instinct is to view them as a human being, not consider what country they come from. I want to go back to something Ed Miliband said earlier on. He said there should be limits on the privatisation of the National Health Service. I'm sorry, Ed, I take a very different view. There should be no privatisation of the Health Service. Our National Health Service is far too precious to give it up for private profit. And to Nick Clegg, you legislated to allow 49% of hospital beds to be used by the private sector. My message to people in England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if there are SNP MPs in the House of Commons after the election and you want to roll back the tide of privatisation, we will be your allies in doing that.

Is that fair? Do you feel, do you sleep happy at that? The public sector has been restrained in its pay to 1% increase. Of course there will be those working within the NHS, nurses and others who will get progression pay increases as well as their 1%.

This is not a US presidential election. We're here to debate the issues in British politics. If you take a step back, why is he here? He's here because of a NATO summit to discuss our security. And that is something that is incredibly important. The relationship we have with America is incredibly important for keeping us safe. The intelligence that is shared keeps all of us safe every single day. And that's not something to turn your nose at. It's something that we should be welcoming. But I'll say this. It is important when we're electing a British prime minister that they are someone who's not able to sustain that relationship, but they are also able to sustain those alliances that keep us safe. NATO is something that I believe and my party believe is crucial to global security. Jeremy Corbyn has described something that is a threat to global security and a danger that should be abolished.

There's still three weeks left in this campaign and all I would say is to anybody that thinks that you can predict the outcome of the election in the middle of the campaign, ask Theresa May how that worked out last time round.

It makes no sense to spend £100 billion when we have so much social need. We must recognise the role that we have played in the past, the decisions that our governments have made, in making the world a much less safe place. Now, Plaid Cymru was strongly opposed to the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, and we've tried hard to hold the former Labour government to account for the decisions that it's made. But we're adamant that our communities can't be made safer just by pursuing defence. A strong diplomatic presence is required, too, in order to promote justice and human rights. We can't justify spending £100 billion on a new generation of weapons of mass destruction in the form of Trident, and so we should pursue a policy instead of reversing our role in the world to be a haven for human rights and a beacon for conflict resolution and peace.
Come down to the picket line with me next time, and we'll try and find someone who says that's why they're leaving. I won't be on the picket line.

I haven't given him any undertakings about anywhere, and I don't make the decision according to what the President of the United States might want. I make it according to what I think are in the interests of this country, and if you look at the military action we've taken, in respect of Kosovo, right, well we could have opted out of that too, but look what happened in the early 1990s when hundreds of thousands of people died in the Balkans. We took military action in Afghanistan, look at the democratic election that's happening there. We took the action in Iraq and I explained that already. We took the action in Sierra Leonie where we saved a country' democracy from a gang of murderous gangsters, you know and, and am I going to do - no, I'm sorry, I, I think we can be proud of what the magnificent British Armed Forces have done, and those decisions, some of them, particularly Iraq, were very difficult.

I think you ask an absolutely key question. I'm not saying everything is perfect. I'm saying we have not finished the work. That's why I'm so keen to do another five years of continuing to get the country back to work, to build a strong economy, to pay down our debts, and to hand on a really good country to our children. That's what this is all about. I'm not saying we fixed it. It takes a long time to fix the mess that I was left to clear up. But what I would say is we're halfway through a building job. Now, you can keep the team that are building the strong economy or you can go back to the team, frankly, the ones that designed the building that fell down in the first place. I think that would be a terrible mistake.

I'm focused on getting our Liberal Democrat policies over so we can get lots of Liberal Democrat tuition fees. That's not a no. Listen, I'm here. I'm enjoying being on your programme so I can get our Liberal Democrat policies over.

Why university fees? I think we should invest in education for the future and I do think that abolishing university tuition fees means that any young person has then got the chance to go to university if they can get in. If you look at the process of applications at the moment, the number of working class students is dropping, the number coming from poorer communities is dropping. I want to see real access for everybody. I think that's the right thing to do. It would not be unusual in many other parts of the world to do this. Listen, as a young person, basically, I had the offer of free education. I didn't take it up. That was my choice, and I'm not complaining about it. I don't think it's up to me and my generation to pull up the ladder on the generations that are coming behind. I want to see an education system that really does work for all, so that every child can develop to their full potential, irrespective of their background and the life chances they were given at birth.

I want us to stay in the EU, Veronica. And as you say, it's not my priority to have a referendum whether we should leave the European Union. Let me explain why. I think when I look at our country, I know what my priorities will be as Prime Minister, to tackle the cost of living crisis, to rescue our NHS, to build a future for our young people. I think leaving the EU will be a disaster for our country because I think we rely on its jobs and the trade we get from being in the EU. I think strategically for Britain, whether you want to tackle terrorism or climate change or a whole range of issues, we've got to be an outward-looking country. And so it's not my priority. Now, I have said, and this is unlikely to happen, but I have said that if there's a transfer of powers from Britain to the EU, a further transfer of powers, we'd have an in-out referendum. But it's not likely to happen. And look, that's what leadership's about, is saying what your priority is. I don't want us to leave the EU, so why would I call a referendum in 2017 and plunge the country into two years of debate about something I don't want to see happen?
Well, it obviously depends on what their earnings are.

No, it's not, sir. Can I just say to you, as I've just said, It would have been the easiest thing in the world for me, having become Prime Minister after the referendum when David Cameron resigned, to say, you know, the next election is not till 2020. This is a good job. I enjoy doing this job. I want to do it. In this job, I do what I believe is the best for Britain. I could have stayed on doing that job for another couple of years and not called an election.

If you're a pensioner, you should know there is a retirement tax coming for you, capital R, capital T, if he is your Prime Minister. For the first time in our country's history, pensioners are going to be paying tax because he won't match our triple lock plus. If I'm your Prime Minister, that won't happen. And that's what I'll deliver for you. As I say it again, do not surrender your finances to their tax rises.
It's heartbreaking. Any war zone is heartbreaking, of course. And the more we can do to bring wars to an end with a negotiated settlement, the better, the faster, clearly.

And for that alone you should apologise. So when you look at where the Labour Party is now compared to where it was, we're in a better place. We will end austerity. We will invest in our communities again. We will end tuition fees. We will give kids the education they deserve. And we'll stop older people dying on trolleys, not getting the support that they need at the moment. That's what Labour's promising now.
Yes.

Because under the Tories, they've frozen the threshold at which you start paying income tax and therefore for too many people, particularly in industries like hospitality and social care, work doesn't pay. That's the reality. That's why if you lift the threshold to £20,000, automatically that's an extra net £1,500. purpose.

because we are, look at what we've done already with nurses, there are 17,000 more nurses now than there were in 2010. And yes, look, if you want my view on this, yes of course I want more GPs. And of course I want more investment in the NHS. And we're putting in now the biggest ever cash boost into the NHS under this One Nation Conservative government. And we will get more GPs, 54 million GP appointments, more GP appointments between now and 2025. We will do that because we are putting the sums, and it's not just that we're building new hospitals, we're investing in people as well. Do you want the answer? The answer is that we're upgrading 20 and as a result of the decisions that we've taken... No, as a result of the decisions that we have taken, in 10 years' time, 40 new hospitals will have been built. And yes, we're starting...
It's a completely hypothetical question. It's a completely hypothetical question. We have to look at the evidence that's there at the time.

Thank you. Well, thank you for your question. The NHS in particular, it's an act of faith for the people of this country. They want to be able to rely on it. But since the COVID pandemic, the caseload that our healthcare professionals are dealing with has gone up by 43%. The only way that we can reduce those waiting lists is to keep the NHS budget strong. That we have done, that we will continue to do. The only party on this platform that has ever cut the NHS budget is Labour. they've got it three times in Wales and we have to increase the number of healthcare professionals that we have. We have been doing that, 70,000 more nurses for example, we have to continue that and we won't do if we can't retain them in the service because they're being taxed out of it because of their pension.

Well, I think that we conducted ourselves constructively, and in the way in which I like to conduct my politics. Not just by name calling, by making the rational case for opposition, and as we now are in this election, maintaining it by being the only one of the three parties saying, with the United Nation's mandate expiring at the end of this calendar year, we should now be working and planning towards the phase withdrawal of the British troops, as part of that occupying force, and bringing our forces home.

I think any voter in Scotland who wants to impact our economy, impact our NHS, should vote for the parties who want to cut migration, which is the Labour Party, the Conservative Party and Nigel Farage. And this is a consequence of us being led down the garden path by the right wing in British politics for far too long. We need to stand against it, we need to promote our economy, promote our public services and to do so by promoting migration.
The figure that you're left with at the moment is £23,000, so we're actually quadrupling that.
You're talking about people lying.
And that's the real problem, isn't it?
Let's put power back in the hands of the Scottish people and take it out of politicians at Westminster. I'm sure the Labour Party would back that premise.

We are committed to scrapping tuition fees. I think they are a barrier to many young people going to university, particularly from poorer backgrounds. We would reinstate student grants for those students from working-class areas. What we would also do is improve vocational training. We would create more vocational schools. As I said earlier, we would look at the German system, whereby you have on-the-job training whilst attending a vocational school. This would give many young people in our country a trade which they can go on throughout their life and apply, whether that's being an electrician or a plumber or a bricklayer, they will learn to do things with their hands. Also, we will have a house-building programme 250,000 affordable homes every year. A brownfield revolution which will allow young people to get on the housing ladder.
Sure.

Mr. Clegg. I think on this matter we do. I don't agree, I've made it publicly clear in the past, that I don't agree with the formal doctrine on homosexuality of the Catholic Church, for instance. I don't agree with it at all. It doesn't mean I don't, you know, I think there should be some you know, uprising against the Pope's visit, quite the reverse. I would like to see the Pope here. I think the Catholic community in Britain wants to welcome him. But, you know, as I think we've all acknowledged, that doesn't mean one has to subscribe to every letter and every cross-T and every dotted I of the theology and doctrine of that church.
This is Nigel telling us he doesn't believe in the NHS. This is what he's doing. He's telling the public he does not believe in the NHS.

Well, Bridget, first of all, thank you for your advocacy for these causes, because they do really matter. And I think making sure we treat retired people with dignity and security in their old age, after a full working life, is one of the most important things we can do as a country. And that's why I've been so clear that the pensioner benefits, like the free TV licences, the free prescriptions, the winter fuel payments, they will continue for everybody. I think it should be your right and we need to make that very clear. I was asked this question the other day about should we have a dedicated older person's representative in the cabinet. I will think about it but I'm a little bit worried about it on this basis. I don't want older people's concerns to be sort of restricted to one person in the cabinet. I want every one of my ministers to be thinking, how do we treat senior citizens properly? I want my health minister to be thinking, how do we treat people in our hospitals and in our care homes? Are we doing this right? I want to make sure that our education minister is thinking about through life education that some people want to continue with forever. I want to make sure that our defence minister is thinking really carefully about veterans and their needs as they grow older. So I'd be worried about possibly having one person and saying they were the only person who thought about older people. In the end, as I said to this meeting the other day, if we're not doing right by older people, don't blame all my men, blame me, because I lead the government and I think we've got to get this right. But pensioners have been absolutely key to this government about uprating pension in line with earnings, prices, or 2.5%, the so-called triple lock. so that pensioners have been protected from some of the difficult decisions we've had to take.

First of all, yes, we are the only party... Your plan is to move out by Christmas? That's right. The UN mandate, the current United Nations mandate for the occupying forces expires at the end of this calendar year. And we have argued, we're the only party arguing this, obviously in the election, that we believe there should now be a phased withdrawal of British troops with that deadline in mind. I think, obviously, we profoundly disagreed with the decision to go to war in Iraq. And I think everybody knows that. We were the only party that opposed it in Parliament. We are where we are. We have got 8,000 British service personnel still out there in Iraq. And I'll tell you what made me angry today, because this issue has been, as you know, in the headlines. When the Prime Minister said this morning that the published advice of the Attorney General, which had to be absolutely dragged from him, we've been calling for this for the last year and a half, represented a damp squib. I say to Tony Blair, go and describe these findings as a damp squib to the families of the British service personnel who gave their lives in Iraq, and to the innocent Iraqi citizens who were killed.

Well, misogyny, racism, discrimination has absolutely no place in our society, but particularly when it comes to the police, because they're people that we look to to protect us and trust. And all the things that we've seen and read about over the past year or two have been really appalling and shocked us to a core, because we thought, hang on, the people that we put our trust in There are a small minority, and we shouldn't tar the entire police force with the actions of a small minority, but a small minority of people who were behaving in a way that was completely reprehensible.

There's no doubt the country is going to have to come together to deal with this really big problem of the deficit. I mean, for every £4 that we spend right now, the government is borrowing one of those pounds. But Nick keeps saying he's being very straight with you. In his manifesto is a promise for a £17 billion tax cut. It's a great idea, I'd love to do it, but we don't have £17 billion for a tax cut.

I've said that I think no deal would be better than a bad deal. Now, I'm confident we can get a good deal with the right plan for those negotiations because I think a good deal is in our interest and in the interest of the rest of the EU. But we have to be prepared to stand up for Britain. We have to be prepared to go in there recognising that we're not willing to accept a bad deal.
without any clarity as to where they're actually going to be.
Okay.

We'd like to, but you're right, and other people like the IFS are right, the money isn't there to do everything we want. So when we publish our manifesto you'll see that actually raising that is a priority when the public finances allow.

Absolutely. So people who take more than three return international flights a year, those frequent flyers would pay more under our plans. And it is those people who take the many, many, many flights that we need to target with those.
What you can say is, here are the number of people that are actually currently applying for asylum

We are one of the only parties here, I think, who want to cancel HS2. And it's the opportunity cost of investing in such a big project when we could be investing in the local sustainable transport that people are crying out for. People are trapped in their cars. They want better buses. They want better local transport. They want to be able to walk and cycle. And that's where we differ on HS2. We like Eurostar. We like high speed rail. But that project at this moment, we could spend that money so much better in other places.

I just want to be very direct. We have taken some people from Syria, some of the most vulnerable people, including elderly and disabled people. But the most important thing we can do in Syria is maintain the fact that we are one of the biggest, I think the second largest, bilateral aid donor helping people in those refugee camps to be fed, to be housed, to be clothed, and try to find a solution so they can go home. There are about six million people or in danger of being refugees, we cannot take all those people in. So it makes sense to use our aid budget to try and help them in the region and then help them get home.

My question is also to Angela Rayner, given the convergence between Labour and the Conservatives on public spending. Maybe I should ask if Labour would accept Penny Mordaunt into your party, but I'll ask this. Gordon Brown said child poverty is a scar on the soul of Britain. Now, is it the likely incoming Labour government's two-child benefit cap or the £18 billion worth of cuts to public services which will make the situation worse?

Well, the WASPI issue, as everybody knows, is a very difficult problem caused by a change in the pension age for women who retire at a certain age. And I do sympathise deeply with the position of the WASPI women. And we have looked at it and looked at it and looked at it. I would love to be able to magic you any solution. It is very expensive to come up with the solution that you want. And I'm going to be honest with you tonight. I cannot promise that I can magic up that money for you tonight. All the demands that the WASP women make. We are going to look at it, but it is not possible to satisfy all the demands of the WASP women. I'm going to put my hands up and confess that.

I think we've discussed this at some length about the aspirations we all have. I do not want to be responsible for the destruction of millions of people, neither do you. Therefore we have to work for a world where they're not available and not used.

Look, if Brexit hadn't happened, do I think right now... I would still support independence, OK? I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But do I think right now we'd be standing here talking about having another independence referendum so imminently? Probably not. But circumstances have changed dramatically. And in a democracy...

And a lot of people make a great deal of money out of the poverty of those people. I am astonished that the Labour Party is now aping UKIP in using the same arguments about immigration. The single European market really matters to all of us right across the UK. And a free movement of people really matters too. Why don't you stand up to the Tories and say we want to remain within the single European market because it matters to jobs of people from Britain and the rest of Europe.

But I would say that actually it's to do with the Conservatives' choices, because at the same time as eroding all of that investment, they've made choices to give a tax cut to the big banks. But on the issue of immigration, I would say the Conservatives have made a complete mess, both of migration system, but also of the asylum system. And we have the worst of both worlds. We have people who are desperate, who are fleeing war and persecution, arriving on small boats. And at the same time, we have our NHS, our social care, we have hospitality and engineering. None of them can recruit the staff that they need with the skills to boost our economy. So Liberal Democrats have said on care in particular, we should give care workers a higher minimum wage, £2 higher, invest in our care workers, give them status, give them progression in the workforce. And then when we still have skills gaps, invite people in from other countries and welcome them when they come.

Paul, that is complete nonsense, if I may say so. Our corporation tax has been consistently lowered by this government with the support of the Liberal Democrats since 2010. What we're proposing is to put it up during the lifetime of Parliament to 26%. That is less than it was in 2010. And if you put that corporation tax up, you're then in a position to deal with the crisis in social care, the crisis in our NHS, properly fund our schools, and not ask headteachers to collect... And I would just say this since Amber Rudd seems so confident that this is a country at ease with itself. Have you been to a food bank? Have you seen people sleeping around our station? Have you seen...

Yes, I think so. I think that fitness and politics isn't just about personal well being, but I think it's fitness in terms of a direct approach with people, established over many years, and I hope people feel I have that.

If you listened to me a few minutes ago, I said that climate change is real. But it's much cheaper to adapt to it than foolishly think you can stop the power of the sun. Net zero is the wrong thing at the wrong price in the wrong time frame. Do you know what it's doing? It's killing our jobs. It's killing our industries like the steel industry, the automotive industry. It's killing our economy. And it's killing old people who can't afford their heating bills.
I don't think so at all.
I'm sorry to shut you down.
You're not answering the question, David, as usual.

Well, you'll have a set of sanctions and incentives, and we will look at the models that are existing around Europe to get the appropriate mix of those. There's a range of different options that exist.

We need to put... We need to put money and resource into Grimsby. We need resource decision-making to come. So we have to be bold enough in government to release that so that there's more power here in Grimsby. They're difficult things to do. Governments haven't done that in the past, but we have to do that. Some of it we can do straightaway. The potholes that are driving everyone mad because their cars get damaged, it costs them hundreds of pounds, and if you need your car or your van for work, it's a complete nightmare because you can't actually use it. That can be done straightaway. We've been setting out our plans on that. Childcare came up this morning in a discussion I was having. We want to create 100,000 new places for childcare. This is at 3,000 nurseries around schools, because I think primary schools are the best place for nurseries. So that means children get the opportunity to develop the skills that they need before reception year, which isn't happening in the way it should at the moment, in my view. But also, parents and carers can go back into work, which many of them want to do, and that then helps the local economy. There are things that we can do. It goes back to my point. We've got first steps, the things we want to do, the day we get into government. If we're elected in, that I want to start, and I've said to my Shadow Cabinet, I want you to prepare months ago for the first day in government. I don't want you picking up the phone on day one. I want to know you're ready. And that's why I'm so confident that we can do this as soon as we get the chance, if we do get a chance, to bring about the change this country so desperately needs.

I'm not going to have a European army. But let me explain. I think I've said that a number of times. Look, let me explain the point about Europe, because Nigel raises it. Think about the problems that we have in the world. Think about the issue of Russia, where we need sanctions to remain in place and, if necessary, to be stepped up. It's Europe and America working together. Think about the battle against ISIS. Look, ISIS is a disgusting, evil organization, and we must give them no quarter and defeat them at home and abroad. But we've got to do it by cooperating across borders, and this is this fundamental point for us as a country. We've got to learn the lessons of the past. We can't have Britain thinking it can solve the problems of the world on its own with the United States. That is the lesson of the 2003 Iraq war. But at the same time, we can't withdraw from the world, because otherwise those problems will visit us here at home.

Can I just remind you that in 2005, Theresa May voted against the anti-terrorist legislation at that time. And she voted against it, as did David Davis, as did a number of people that are now in your cabinet, because they felt that that legislation was giving too much executive power. My opposition to anti-terror legislation isn't opposition to protecting us from terrorism, it is simply saying there must be judicial oversight over what is done in our name. You cannot give...
...the thing, it will depend on the local circumstances.

Well, the facts are clear. Under the rules that we passed in 2012, this person would not be out of prison. And we have actually invested more in counter-terrorism and in the police. But the key is this. It's not just about resources. It's about powers. We want to do more stop and search, Labour opposes that. We want to do tougher sentencing, Labour opposes that. These are the things that will make a difference in our security.

Just answer that. Yes, because at the moment, we've had flat growth and really poor growth in this country. And we're not going to be able to tax our way out of the disastrous situation that has left us in. But what we are going to do is we're going to have a growth strategy with an industrial strategy, with a skills strategy, which will enable us to grow the economy so that we can pay for our public services. We can't tax our way out of this. We've got the highest tax burden. Exactly what you were going to do.

Yeah, so Big Stick we were hearing about, is that what you're going to give? So we need to have resources. I mean, Cressida Dick said today that she did not favour and didn't think it would make us a safer country if we armed every officer in the UK. I agree with her. But that doesn't mean we shouldn't have more resources. And of that £300 million the Liberal Democrats would give to our police forces, some of that would, of course, go to ensuring we did have that capability. There's a question about people being allowed back in the country. And that's a really good question, given that we had a question about the powers that we do or don't need. So the government has, the Home Secretary has, the power to issue temporary exclusion orders. In the last two years, she's used one. One. In those two years. And we know of people who potentially could have been stopped. It's not, do we have the powers? It's, do we use the powers?

Killing somebody actually happened outside Parliament a month ago. Yes. And I think you have to look at all the evidence that's there. But you can't answer a hypothetical question without the evidence around it.

Yeah, let me do something that I don't very often do. Oh, no. No, don't be worried. Praise Boris Johnson. Wow. I didn't agree with him on lots of things, and I thought the way he conducted himself in Partygate was a disgrace and he got kicked out of Parliament. But to be fair, On Ukraine, he took a strong position. I was leader of the opposition, and I said we will support the government. And so there's been unity in Parliament from the outbreak of that conflict and Russia aggression there. And I thought that was important, Boris Johnson thought it was important, because I think we both took the view that the only winner, if there was a split in our politics here in the UK, would be Putin. He wanted to see division in other countries, and we didn't allow him. So, I do think... And I've also supported Rishi Sunak on this, and Liz Truss for the brief time that she was Prime Minister. And I still continue to do so. So, even where there might be small disagreements between us, I think it's in the national interest, the country's interest, that we speak with one voice on something as important as Ukraine. And we will continue to do so, and I've had the opportunity to meet President Zelensky, I went to Kiev to see him, to say, should there be a change of government, there won't be any change in the support for Ukraine, because I think it's fundamental for Ukraine and for our own freedom here in this country.
Yes.

Absolutely, Adam. I wholeheartedly agree. This is an opportunity for us as a country to take a real lead.
