And if you look at what the great success story has been in the last few years, if we were having this debate six or seven years ago, people were saying renewables, they're really expensive and they won't be so reliable. there was no offshore wind. We are now in the UK a leader, world leader, in offshore wind. It is now the cheapest way to generate electricity. Because of the support for that industry that was put in by the Liberal Democrats when we were running the climate change department, that has created a great British industry. It's cutting energy bills. That's a great example of what we need to
Are you talking to me or Rishi? Thank you. You don't need to be here, Rishi, because that is exactly... What's your plan, Angela? This is an election campaign. What's your plan?

Yeah, well, we've said in the manifesto, in the costings, we've pointed to a number of tax rises, and I'm talking about the one of clamping down on people who aren't paying their fair share of tax. The way to do that is to have more tax inspectors. Her Majesty's revenue customs have been cut back dramatically, and they can't do the job. And so if you talk to the experts, they reckon for every pound you spend, in tax officials and the technology for HMRC, you can raise £18. Now, we haven't put that in our costings. We've just said an extra £7. So I think we've been quite cautious. and I think the money can be found. It's about the political will. And look what you can do with it. Our NHS is on its knees. It's on its knees. People can't get GPs. There's no NHS dentist. They're having to wait hours for ambulances. And on the care system, people are going without care. That is just wrong.

Well, our deal actually allows the United Kingdom to leave the EU intact as one UK and respect the result of the referendum. What would be bad for the Union is to spend years more arguing about referendums on Brexit again and on Scotland. That would be divisive. It would not bring people together. And maybe if I could pose a question, because hearing about all these deals, Nicola, if you are going to be ending up propping Jeremy Corbyn in number 10, and we have all these second referendums, which one will you insist comes first, the one on Scotland or the one on Brexit?

We've got to resolve them. And for months and months and months, the Prime Minister hasn't resolved them. They've been arguing about who gets in the room. You've got to get in the room, you've got to resolve it, because the NHS needs to get back to work. But we've also got to bring down the waiting list. We've got a plan. for 40,000 extra appointments each and every week. That's 2 million a year to bring those waiting lists down. They're at a record high. And under the last Labour government, the record was waiting lists right down, satisfaction in NHS at a record high. That's completely been turned on its head.
No.

Well, I would add to the long list of criticisms that Caroline has put out before us that there are now 800,000 fewer households that have workless households in them. We have focused on making sure that people can get into their work. But work doesn't pay numbers. Work doesn't pay numbers. Caroline, Caroline, we need to have a system. You cannot ignore the fact that over the past seven years, there have been another 3 million people that have got into jobs. You attack the reductions in corporation tax. You talk about corporations. I talk about jobs. What we need to have is the investment to make sure that people can get those jobs, so they can have the dignity of the jobs, so they can feed their family, and they can work and achieve positive jobs.

Well, firstly, I want to say again that my thoughts are with everybody affected on London Bridge today with those who sustained injuries and I think in particular the two families this evening who are facing up to the loss of a loved one. We witnessed today some exceptional acts of bravery on the part of members of the public and I, like I'm sure all of us are, are full of admiration. It's how all of us hope we'd respond in a situation like that but in truth none of us know until we're confronted by it and hopefully we will not be in that position. In terms of what we do, we have to make sure that we support our security services, that we keep access to the best intelligence, which is one of many reasons that I think leaving the European Union is a mistake. And although the gentleman is right, no amount of police officers can prevent every terrorist attack. the numbers of police officers is important in ensuring the speed of response we saw from the emergency services today. The SNP government in Scotland has over the past decade increased and maintained police numbers. I think it is regrettable that hasn't happened elsewhere in the UK and I hope after this election we will see police officers get the support that they so richly deserve.

Well Martin, as a serving police officer obviously you'll be aware that we have police and counter-terrorism police. We've been very clear that we have protected the budgets for counter-terrorism policing and we're currently protecting overall police budgets as well. And we're ensuring that we're putting money in to enable the police to address what are actually different ways, as you will know, different types of crime. Crime is changing. And policing will be changing in the future. It's why we're putting more money, for example, into cyber policing, because increasingly we see those as the sort of crimes that are having to be addressed. We did do what was called Operation Temperer, which enabled the military to assist the police following the terrible, horrific attack that took place in Manchester. That's been a well-prepared plan for some time, and that was put into operation and put into operation smoothly and is now being run down. What is important is that we not only ensure we have the counter-terrorism police we need. and the overall police that we need, but also that they have the powers that they need. And that's what I've done as Home Secretary. I ensured that we brought through new legislation to put powers into place, which gave the police what they were asking for in order to be able to deal with counter-terrorism.
Leader but when we're in government we'll negotiate.
No no, that was, that was for asylum seekers. That - that for genuine refugees  BOTH TOGETHER
Let me No but it is when we'll get in to government, and let me  BOTH TOGETHER
That's not true.

The Labour Party signed the treaty setting up NATO. If you go to Brussels... We were founding members of NATO, which I fundamentally believe in, in terms of our national security. who ever has been a leader of the Labour Party, including Jeremy Corbyn, I have never let them move away from NATO. So we never veered away from NATO. We needed to have people in and fighting for the future of our party. Now, some people were on the back benches, some were on the front benches, but we were all in one fight, which was to make sure we had a Labour Party at the capable of winning an election in the future. I had those battles on later.

Any circumstances where anyone's prepared to use a nuclear weapon is disastrous for the whole planet. That is why there has to be a policy of disarmament globally, but through a multilateral policy, not a unilateral policy.
You wouldn't walk out. We will be there to negotiate the right deal, but what I have said is no deal is better than a bad deal. We have to be prepared to walk out.

Hang on. Hold on. A city the size of Hull came to this country, net. That's going to be a Birmingham over a five-year period. It's unsustainable. What we need to do is we need to reduce immigration and we do it by having an Australian points-based system. So if you've got the skills that this country needs, yes, please come here and work. But beyond that, we have to get the population under control. Because if we carry on on the roads we're on, we'll have a population of 80 million by the middle of this century. Now you just think what will happen. There'll have to be a huge school building programme. There'll have to be new hospitals, new motorways, a new rail network, new houses. We're already having to build a house every seven minutes simply to keep up with the numbers of people coming to this country. Reduce immigration, have a points-based system. It will be good for the economy, and it will be good for social cohesion.

Well, just back to Johnny. Look, of course you need to deal with debts and deficit. If you don't, you simply make Johnny's generation and our kids and our grandkids pick up the sort of... ..pick up the bill for this generation's mistakes. It's how do you do it? And you've basically got the following option. You either borrow too much money, which is Labour's plan, or you cut too much, which is the Conservatives' plan. And when you cut, of course you do need to make savings, and no-one should deny that. But it's very important why you do it. And the difference between David Cameron and myself is, I believe you need to make the necessary cuts because you have to. George Osborne and David Cameron make much further cuts than is needed because they want to. And that's a huge difference.
Well, we already committed that we'd have mental health services in schools. That's one of our commitments.

We have said we will consult on that but I can tell you very clearly that the billionaires will not be given it. But Jeremy has particularly asked me about looking after pensioners and I would like to answer that. We will always look after pensioners, ensuring they have the dignity and security in retirement. And we have made sure, we have committed to making sure, that their pension will always be uprated according with earnings or inflation. That is so different to the commitment they got under Labour.

Let me just answer that quickly, sorry, forgive me. Have you looked at shared ownership as well because that's another thing that you can look at where you pay part rent, but eventually end up sharing the ownership. Housing associations in my area of orbit do a lot of that. They operate in other areas, but it's worth looking at as well.

Well, Julie, first of all, thank you for doing what you do. My mum was also a small business owner. I grew up in a family that had a family business. I know how hard it is. I know what the struggles are like. And our economy is built on people like you. And you deserve all our support and admiration and praise for doing what you are doing. I'm sorry things have been difficult. We had the referendum, and now our focus should be on making sure we make the best of all the opportunities. In an economy-wide sense, we're actually doing very well now at exporting around the world. But in order to help you, I have two specific things that I can do that will help. One of them is to make sure that we cut your taxes on business rates. When I talk to small retailers like yours, I think you said you had a premises, the thing that everyone talks to me about is business rates. That's why we've cut business rates for small retail restaurants, pubs, shops across the country and Labour on Wales, they haven't done that, so everyone's taxes are going up.

Ask yourself this. Do you want Brexit sorted in weeks or more referendums taking years? Focus on the NHS, schools and police or more arguing about Brexit? To move on or to stay gridlocked? This is the choice you have. Boris Johnson delivering Brexit by the end of January and focusing on your priorities. Or Jeremy Corbyn ridiculously stuck in neutral on one of the biggest questions our country has ever faced. Only the Conservatives can break this deadlock and set Britain free.

Well, pensioners are not going to be better off with a Prime Minister who's making promises that he can't keep because they're not funded. That's exactly what Liz Truss got wrong. There's one thing that we cannot repeat at this election. Please do not go back to Liz Truss mark two with this Prime Minister because the same damage will follow.

There is no answer to your question because nobody could answer at this stage what I've said. Well, you don't know. I've said what we would do is pay that we are legally required to do. There will have to be an agreement on trade access. Now, it's not a one-way street because all of our manufacturing industry relies on supply chains across Europe, as indeed many European manufacturers supply chains from this side of the channel as well.

the, the legal case was the, the breach of United Nations resolutions, the evidence from the Iraq Survey Group, is that he was indeed in breach of UN resolutions, and incidentally, it wasn't just us in Britain that concluded. No, hang on a minute. It wasn't just us in Britain that concluded he had WMD, the entire United Nations concluded that, which is why they passed resolution 14.41.

cut the deficit. You have said what you are going to do and that is fine. The question we started off with, how can they believe your promises? You have failed in this government to eliminate the deficit. That was your promise. It is running at £90 billion. Tell Johnny who asked the question, what are you going to cut?

I don't think that's likely. I think we've got to bring people with us on this journey. Public attitudes have changed enormously in the last 10 years, but it's important to keep people with us. We're making massive changes as a society. We've decarbonized as a country. What do you do? In lots of different things I do. There are many things I do. I've made efforts to make my home more energy efficient. I've used less plastics. I've done more insulation at home. I do try and drive less, use more public transport. I think we're all trying to do... These are small things, admittedly, but we're all trying to do more in our daily lives. And in politics, as a government, we've done a huge amount. We are decarbonising faster than any of the other major countries in the world. We've legislated now for net zero by 2050. I know some people would like to go further than that, but that was a huge step forward.

They have to... We have to have a scheme in place to stop them driving the rent up and up and up. OK, I'm still not entirely clear how you're going to do that. Well, no, no, but the other thing I do want to address is this, which is deposits... Lots of renters are paying huge deposits, and we have to stop that as well and make sure the conditions are good within the housing they're buying. But at the end of the day, there's no way out of the renting problem until we build more houses, because without the houses that people can afford to live in and buy, then more people are renting, and that is bingo for landlords who are making a fortune out of this.

Women's spaces must be protected. It's very, very important. And again, when I was chief prosecutor, I was working with very many people who had been victims of sexual violence and domestic violence. It was a big part of the work I was doing. And I saw for myself how important those women's spaces are, and they must be protected. whether it's in that area, whether it's single-sex wards or sport, that must be protected. We mustn't go down the route of self-identification, but we do need to recognise the procedure or the process at the moment is not dignified. There are some people who don't identify with the gender that they're born into. They are very distressed by that. And my rule in life is to treat everybody with respect, and dignity, and that's what I will do, but we must protect safe spaces.

Nick Clegg is promising a £17 billion tax cut. Now, we're saying stop the waste of £6 billion to stop the national insurance rise. I would love to take everyone out of their first £10,000 of income tax, Nick. It's a beautiful idea. It's a lovely idea. We cannot afford it. Shall I tell you how we pay for it?
No. We're going to cut taxes

Thank you. First of all, I have to defend my party. We very, very deliberately throughout this campaign have not done the smearing. We've not done the name calling. And so what you're saying about Theresa May, what we're doing is we're looking at her record over the last seven years, six of which she was the Home Secretary, so in charge of police, in charge of our security. And I think it's acceptable to look at policies and look at decisions that politicians make. But I completely agree, it's totally unacceptable to name call. To go back to the bigger picture, I would say in the last five years, it's become much more acceptable to use really despicable language, which I would actually say is getting very close to hate crimes on social media. Politicians are using it, posters that we're seeing. I have to say the Brexit campaign was a particularly vile example of that. I'm glad that it feels in this election that that's calmed a little bit, but we need to have zero tolerance to that sort of thing. To look at the big picture, it's not just about a knee-jerk reaction of bringing in more police, bringing in more legislation. What we need to be doing is, from the youngest age, is teaching people about respect, about tolerance, and embedding that in. and then bringing back things like the youth service. You know, the youth service has been decimated in the last seven years. That's the place where young people can be, you know, just sort of channeled a little bit if they're saying things which are too upsetting, controversial, radical. That system isn't there now. So we're seeing that the schools are under-resourced, we're seeing the youth services disappeared, we're seeing the police, the community police aren't there. So all of those levels, which would have been like our canaries, when things were starting to go wrong, we could have gone, oops, hang on, we've got an issue here, let's put some support around that person. With all that gone, it becomes an inevitability that it's only when someone does something so utterly despicable, and I'm really sorry for the people that are directly impacted on what's happened in these last three, actually, atrocities. We shouldn't just be waiting until that moment. We should be doing something decades before.

You're absolutely right that trust in politics is broken. And the Liberal Democrats have always said it's not just about who's in power, it's about what you do with power. So we believe in devolving power to local communities so they have more say over what they believe in. And we want proportional representation so that every single vote of every single person actually counts in a general election.
Well, let's just be very, very clear. It's only thanks to the Brexit Party that Brexit is happening still at all. That's the first thing. I mean, the Tories three times...

I got into politics to fight, to stand up to those who take you for granted. I grew up in Preston in the 1980s. I saw what happens when decent people are taken for granted by a heartless Conservative government and I am determined to stop that happening again. The decent Britain I love is under threat. Theresa May, backed by Nigel Farage and Jeremy Corbyn, is going for an extreme Brexit that will damage our futures for generations. Don't give up. The Britain I love is not lost yet. No matter how you voted in the referendum, if you care for our children, don't cut our schools. If you care for our elderly, don't leave them on trolleys in corridors. If you want Britain to lead the world, do not turn your back on the world. A brighter future is still possible. The fight is not over. It is time for some hope.

And the argument you've made, Leanne, is that David Cameron has cut the Welsh budget so much that that has led to cuts in Wales. And the truth is the NHS is struggling in Scotland because Nicola hasn't met her A&E targets for five and a half years. It's got challenges in Wales. It's got challenges across the country. The answer is have a proper funding plan for our national health service.
It's not Labour's policy to have a four-day week in the National Health Service.

On the top level of tax, we have the power to set the rate. What we don't have power over in Scotland are the rules around tax avoidance. So we don't have the ability to set rules that would stop people moving their income south of the border or moving their income into capital gains. So we took advice, and the advice was that if that was introduced in Scotland alone, then we would risk not raising more revenue, but actually seeing a decline in the revenue we raised. So that's why we didn't do it in Scotland alone. I think it should be done across the UK and if it's done in the rest of the UK we would do that here in Scotland as well because we could be more confident then of raising extra revenue for our schools and hospitals. There's no point setting a tax if you're going to come out with less money at the end of it.

David Cameron carried it in his pocket and produced it. I think it's his regular prop. Let me directly address you, Elizabeth. There was a global financial crisis. There was a high deficit. That deficit hasn't been cleared. It will be the mission of my government to cut the deficit every year and balance the books. And let me tell you how I'm going to do it. First of all, we'll have fair taxes. So we'll reverse David Cameron's tax cut for the richest in our society, millionaires. I can't justify a £43,000 tax cut for every millionaire in Britain. Secondly, we'll protect key areas like health and education, but outside those key areas there will be falls in spending because we've got to get the deficit down. But the final thing concerns your living standards. We've seen living standards fall over this Parliament. As a result, lower living standards means lower tax revenues for government, and that's why the deficit hasn't been cleared. So it's a three-part plan to make sure we balance the books.

That's not how it works. That's not how it works. What I'm saying is if it's introduced in the rest of the UK, we would introduce it in Scotland as well. I want to have full powers over tax in Scotland, but right now, if we do it in Scotland alone, because we couldn't prevent people shifting their incomes, then we'd run the risk of losing money. And surely, common sense tells you, if the advice says that you might lose money by putting a tax up, it would not be the most sensible thing to do.

Well I think you've heard it all from a Prime Minister who's just got absolutely nothing left positive to say. 13 years of economic failure, 13 years, sadly, of quite a lot of educational failure, 13 years in which inequality's got worse, in which deep poverty's got worse, in which they haven't got to grips with the problems. That's what you hear. Let me give you some positive things that we would that would make a difference. We've got to get the basics right. I'm very clear that teaching children to read and write using the old fashioned synthetic phonics method, that works best. Setting by ability so you accept that not every child's the same, let's actually stretch the brightest pupils and help those falling behind. That can make a big difference. I had a big argument this week with someone on the pavement about special needs education. I want every child to have a choice between special needs education and mainstream education, but, please, let's stop closing the special schools that do so much for families in our country. Education is about the basics, yes. It's also about then aspiration, saying to every child, no matter where they come from, you can go all the way according to your talent. That's what education should be like, and that's what it would be under our Government.
No, it isn't true.
I'm being prepared to walk away. You've clearly never negotiated... Well, why not ask me? I'm a businessman. I've negotiated deals over three decades.

Why would we trust Boris Johnson, first of all, has been telling us that there are going to be 40 hospitals that turn out to be six, that there are going to be 50,000 nurses that turn out to be more like 30,000 if we're lucky. The fact is that in those documents, if the British government had wanted to, it could have explicitly said the NHS is not up for negotiations, just like the US did on issues around climate change. It chose not to do so. So we are absolutely right to say that the NHS is at risk here.
I wanted there to be a UN-brokered plan of a ceasefire at that time, which President Terry of Peru was trying to pursue.
...it is relevant to the - I can't tell you how many because I don't even know how many potential applicants would be in the pipeline in another five years...
No, I'm setting out the direction of travel.

No, because we touched on it earlier. When I got this job, my number one priority was to get the economy in a strong position. And that's what we've been able to do over the last 18 months, get inflation back to target, get the economy growing, wages rising, energy bills falling. So this is the moment for all of you to decide what future you want for our country. and I want you to have that opportunity, and I'm very passionate and confident in what I'm saying. I believe what I'm saying is right for our country and right for all of you. I think you do want all your taxes cut, because you all work very hard. You deserve to keep more of your money. I want everyone to own their own home, like Rehan, so I want to cut taxes on him. We haven't heard from, I think, any pensioners tonight, but if I'm not your Prime Minister for the first time in history, pensioners are going to face a retirement tax. on their state pension in this country if I'm not your Prime Minister. And we're going to create the triple lock plus to make sure that doesn't happen. Harry, in the next Parliament, if I'm not your Prime Minister, every pensioner is going to be paying a retirement tax for the first time in this country's history. That won't happen if I'm your Prime Minister. If I'm not your Prime Minister, you're all going to be paying thousands of pounds to upgrade your home, switch out your boiler, change your car, because Keir Starmer is going to reverse all the changes I made so we get to net zero in a sensible way. Those illegal migrants will not be on planes to Rwanda. They will be out on our streets, putting pressure on public services. And by the way, I can tell you now, they are queuing up in Calais, waiting for a Starmer government so they can come here and stay here.

Well, what we showed when we were in government, we massively raised it. We promised to do that, and when resources allowed, we did it massively. And what's been so unfair, and there's a huge difference between us and the Conservatives, who really aren't fit to govern our country, you know. They've raised taxes on the lowest income people. We cut taxes on the lowest income people.
I think it's pretty obvious what a Scottish nationalist would demand.
Well, we'd have to work with other countries.
I want real, genuine, radical... Yeah, but you want to get out of the European Union, but you're still happy to go and take the salary as an MEP. Well, if I wasn't doing it... Well, hang on.
Well, it's not going to be done in the first two years, Fiona. We're not saying that.

You have to believe me, you can go and check as a result of their financial mismanagement. The same thing happened in Nottingham, right? That's what you get with the Labour Party. You remember what happened last time they were in office? They run out of your money, they bankrupt the country, and you all pay for it in higher taxes. And that is the choice for you at this election. We are making progress. The economy is growing. Taxes are now coming down. The financial security that you want, I can deliver. But if we put all that progress at risk with them, your family finances are going to get hammered. Your taxes are going to get whacked up. And that is the choice for you at this election. Do not surrender to their tax rises.
Yes or no? The position on the state pension that we've set out is exactly... It's a yes or no?

It's a lie because it makes it sound that you could do it in a matter of weeks or 11 months maximum. It's going to take us years to negotiate a new trade agreement. It took seven years with Canada. And so the idea you're going to get it done through this is just simply a fantasy.

What we had to do when we came into government in 2010 was to ensure that we were living within our means and that was very important because of the economic situation we'd inherited. Now what we need to do in policing terms is ensure it's not just about the numbers of police, people often focus on the numbers of police, it's actually about what the police are able to do and how they are being deployed on our streets. As I say In counter-terrorism policing we have protected those budgets and we're currently protecting police budgets. But crime is changing and the sort of job that the police officers need to do is changing. That's why we're also putting extra money into things like cybercrime because that's today's world. So a government has to ensure that we're keeping abreast with the changes that take place with the challenges that there are out there that we need to deal with. And of course the terrorist threat is severe. That's where it is at the moment. But that's why we ensure that our police, our security service, have that counter-terrorism budget. We are increasing the budgets for our security services as well. But also that they have the powers they need to do the job.
...what, no that must be the decision for the judge, sitting properly in a court to make that decision. Not the decision for me as the politician. That's a very slippery slope to go down.
26, I think.

Coming back to the original point as well, the Tories promised to end Section 21, no full evictions. They haven't. And that's sent homelessness soaring, especially for families. And we also have said we need to make work pay so that people that one of the most scandalous things I've seen, I've talked about my childhood when my mum and dad was on the gyro, we didn't have to go to a food bank. The people using food banks now are actually in work.

I think it's an absolute scandal that 500,000 kids in this country go to primary school and are in classes with over 30 other children in them, considering that Tony Blair gave a commitment that there would be no class sizes over 30 about 14 years ago. I agree with Tim. I would do away with SATs for infants, school-age children. Also, the problem that we have is so many teachers are leaving the profession. And they're leaving the profession because they're overworked. And they're overworked under the deluge of paperwork, schemes of work, individual lesson plans. What you need to do is to strip the paperwork back, strip back the bureaucracy, and let our teachers do what they do best, which is teach our young people to the best of their ability.

If I look at one of the things that we're taking forward in Scotland today, we've got what's called a tech scaler programme, which is designed to take new emerging companies that have emerged from our universities and partnerships with business, to essentially support them and to fuel the growth of those individual companies. And what that's about doing is taking new, fresh, dynamic ideas, the ideas of the future. There are things that are happening today in Scotland. We've got a facility up in Shetland which is now going to start launching satellites by rocket into space. Now, if you'd said to me a few years ago, Scotland will be a satellite-launching country, I would have said, OK, that's quite a big ask, but, you know, it'll be interesting if it happens. It's now happening. So innovation that comes from our universities can be fuelled with public investment and that's what the Scottish Government is doing to support that activity, to strengthen the manufacturing base of Scotland and to strengthen the economic opportunities that we face. Does that answer your question?

I'm not keen on giving Theresa May personally very much, but that's another matter altogether. The SNP did some work some months back, where we set out five different options to compensate the WASPI women. It went from restoring the pension age to 60 to slowing down the pace of change. We certainly would want to talk to other parties to make sure that that justice is delivered, because this is the question. You know, these big parties, I think we've seen tonight a glimpse of the dreadful choice between these two main parties that people have. But they can always find things for the wrong priorities. Let's make a change and find things for the right priorities in compensating WASP women. I think it's a top one.

This is an absolute invention. It is completely untrue. There are no circumstances, whatever, in which this government or any conservative government will put the NHS on the table in any trade negotiation. Our NHS will never be for sale. And I'm amazed how often this comes up. The only reason that it comes up is because Mr. Corbyn is trying to conceal the void at the heart of his Brexit policy and refusing to answer the question of which side, which side he would take. Because the public still, the public have a right, the public have a right to know.

It varies from one local authority area to another. Of course, but on average in the South East as a whole? Well, the average figure for the country as a whole is £450 savings per household. But you're right to look at our website. That's why we've made all this available, and I'd encourage people to watch it. Mr. Kennedy. Yes, I do. Let's make your point again.

Yeah, look, I'm out and about across the country all the time, and I very much sense people's frustrations. Frustrations with me, with the party, and just generally, it's been a very difficult few years. We've had Covid, we have to recover from that. Then we had a war in Ukraine, the impact on people's bills. Of course it hasn't been easy for anyone. And I actually got this job as a result of something that was a mistake. I was very clear about it on the first day I came into this office. when I acknowledge those mistakes, and I was here partly to bring back economic stability, and I'm glad that we've done that. But the key now at this election is about the future. As frustrated as people might be, this is not a by-election, this is not a referendum on the past, this is about the future, and the choice for everyone is clear. I want to build on the progress that we've made, keep cutting everyone's taxes, give people more financial security. I don't want their taxes to go up under a future Labour government. I want to crack on and keep reducing migration, get to net zero in a sensible way. These are all things I believe in, all things I want to deliver, and that's the future that I want to be talking about.

Obviously, it is a very good and relevant question for everybody across the UK. The SNP may well hold the balance of power in a hung Parliament after this election. Let me say, we will use that influence, if we have it, responsibly and in the interests, of course, of standing up for Scotland, but also in the interests of progressive values for people right across the UK. Let me be very honest, I could not, in good conscience, ever put Boris Johnson into number 10 Downing Street. because I have seen the misery that Tory policies have heaped upon people. Jeremy Corbyn may not be my choice of Labour leader but I don't get to choose the Labour leader. In terms of what I would seek to win from a minority Labour government, obviously I would ask for and expect Jeremy Corbyn to respect the right of the Scottish people to choose their own future. Because it is not for Westminster to decide, it is for the people of Scotland. But I would also want an end to austerity. I would want to see an end to the misery of universal credit. I would want to see an NHS protection bill so that our NHS cannot be put at the mercy of Donald Trump in a trade deal. I would want to see fairer pensions for our older people. The UK has one of the lowest pensions in the OECD. So these are the progressive policies that the SNP would champion, yes, for people in Scotland, but also for the benefit of people right across the UK.

There are two immediate things that we should be doing. We should be doing, as a country, we should be leading the way globally with a worldwide initiative to plant hundreds and hundreds of millions of trees to capture CO2. The next thing we should do is we need to invest in major technology, green technology, so that we can capture CO2 and reduce CO2 emissions. There's one more thing that we don't talk enough about, and that is recycling our own waste. My son in Asia, he rang me up in horror when he saw, and he was clearing up, piles and piles of plastic bottles on beaches in Asia, and where did they come from? Supermarkets in Britain. We have to recycle our own waste.

We're proposing £3 billion a year extra for dentistry to enable everyone to get access to an NHS dentist. Where I am in East Anglia, we have a dental desert, just as so much of our country does. People are resorting to pulling out their own teeth, or to going without treatment, or for driving on hours on end to get to an NHS dentist. But this costs a lot of money. You talk about taxing the top 1%. We're saying that we're willing to put the money

Fiona, it's a very fair point. Can I take you through the GP policy? In a manifesto, we say we need to recruit 8,000 more GPs. And we've talked to health experts about how you could do that. It would take about four years, with a billion pounds a year. That's in our cost manifesto. And there's several ways you're going to do it. At the moment, actually, there are people who have been trained at taxpayer's expense and qualified as doctors. and they can't work as GPs because there's not the money there to employ them. How crazy is that? And we need to train more, we need to retain more. GPs are leaving. Some people in our country, they come out of our medical school and they go to Australia. Because in Australia they're better valued, they're better paid.

You keep talking about your workforce strategy. What are you doing to deal with the emigration, the people leaving from our health and social care because they're not being properly paid for the work they do?

On the contrary, we have a fantastic deal. It is there, ready to go. What our deal allows is for British businesses of all kinds to have perfect confidence in the way we leave the EU, because we are in a state of equivalence with zero tariff, zero quota, exact regulatory framework, and we have ample time to get on and build a new free trade partnership, not just with the EU, but with countries around the world. And the great beauty of this deal is that it gets Brexit done, gets it out of Parliament, stops the wrangling, stops the chaos, and moves us forward.

If you want change, you have to vote for it. I say, vote for what you believe in. You don't have to go on voting for the lesser of two evils. That's how we ended up with the tired, failed politics that we have now. If you want a fair economy, a public NHS, a stable climate, vote for change. Vote Green. Already in Parliament, we've seen Caroline Lucas make a huge impact. We need more MPs like Caroline. With a strong group of Green MPs, we can deliver a new kind of politics. You can deliver a peaceful political revolution. Wherever you are in England, Wales, Scotland or Northern Ireland, if you're thinking about voting Green, Do it. Your vote will count.

Well, there is no chance of Britain giving up its seat on the United Nations Security Council. We are stronger. We've just been chairman of the G20 as a result of the efforts we made in the economy. We're leading the negotiations on climate change and we're leading on nuclear weapons.
We will be able to introduce rules for people coming from within the European Union once we've left. That's been a piece of the work on immigration that hasn't been available to us.
I agree, it's only £100 a year to begin with.
I, no point in speculating on that. What I do know is that

I think in the last two years we've seen the introduction, since I became Prime Minister, of foreign national ID cards. So anybody who comes into the country now will have to have an identity card. We're also introducing, from the end of the year, a control so that everybody is counted in and everybody is counted out. It was got rid of in the past. It should not have been got rid of. We are bringing that back. Biometric visas mean that we can stop people at airports, even before they come into the country, if they've not got the right identification. So we're tightening up on that. But the big key to the future is the points system. If you don't have the points to come into this country and otherwise if you don't have a skill we need, don't come to the country. But I must say I am very worried about Nick's policy because it sends a message to people all around the world if you come to Britain there will be some sort of amnesty that will allow you to come here freely in the end without having to be thrown out of the country.

You know, you really should be ashamed of doing things like that. David, I have not authorised any leaflets like that. What I would ask you to say is why is it not in your manifesto that you're keeping free eye tests and free prescription charges? And to Nick I would say, if you're cutting the winter fuel allowance, that means that some people will suffer this year We've already said that all pensioners over 60, all households, will get £250 as the winter fuel allowance and £400 if you're over 80. Now that's an innovation we brought in during the period of the Labour government. I would not like to see it cut in one of the most difficult years for pensioners when we're just coming out of recession, but you seem to be cutting it.

Yeah, so I didn't catch... Rachel. Rachel. Rachel. You know, probably the easiest thing I can tell you is that I have two young daughters. And that's why you can trust me, because as a dad, first and foremost, rather than a politician or prime minister, I want them to grow up in a country which is safe, for them when they become teenagers and can walk around on their own, making sure that we have the right police on the streets, that our communities are safe for women and girls is really important to me. I want to make sure that they have the health that they need and that's why we've been making changes to things like HRT and maternity care in hospitals, but I also want to make sure that when they grow up and have jobs and start a family that they're supported too. which is why we're rolling out 30 hours of free childcare for every working parent from the time that their little one is nine months old to when the toddler goes off to school at four years, and that will make a big difference to families up and down the country. But that's why I said, so I can go into lots more policies, but I want my girls to grow up in a country where the sky is the limit for their ambitions. There is nothing that they can't do because they're women, and that's the kind of place that I believe in.
We would talk to them. We wouldn't necessarily accept what they say, we would have a dialogue with them; that's what consultation means. We want to find out what they think.

What we saw in immigration figures was it started to come down, then they did go up and now they're starting to come down again. What we will have when we leave the EU is the... Yes. Jeremy, I'm not sitting here saying it wasn't. I'm accepting that what we had to do was to ensure that we were rooting out abuse in the system. But there is no single moment where you take one measure which changes the immigration figures. It is a constant work to ensure that you're dealing with immigration and ensuring there is no more abuse in the system. There's more work to be done. We will, of course, have another tool to do this when we leave the European Union, because we'll be able to introduce rules for people coming from within the European Union. But this will be a constant work.
Ask the electorate here.

There are more nurses, more doctors, more people being treated and that's because we have a strong economy. He talks about failure. Let's remember the failure of the last Labour government that virtually bankrupted this country and left us with impossibly difficult decisions. But the biggest decision we made was to protect the NHS and to spend more every year, a decision that Labour at the time said was irresponsible. That's what they said. We ignored that and we went ahead and invested in our NHS as part of a balanced plan for our country.
Yes.

Well, look, I would say to everybody at home that use your vote at this election as a weapon to fight for the future of our National Health Service, because it needs to be rescued from you, David. What about mid-staffs? Because I've got to say, over 13 years, the Labour government transformed an NHS from 18-month waiting to 18-week waiting. That's our record. And now it's going backwards under you. You've failed the British people. You've broken your bond of trust on the National Health Service. They believed you. They believed you. you. They believe you're another kind of Conservative and it's gone backwards on your watch and they won't trust you again.

If I can speak to Simon, can I just say it's every parent's worst nightmare that their child might have a knife pulled on them. The Conservatives have tried to ban zombie knives three times in the last few years and it's clear that that isn't working. So we do need a different approach. We have to have expert youth services that can go in and support young people who are scared in their communities. We have got to have community policing so that police officers know their communities. They work with faith groups, community groups, Neighbourhood Watch, all those kind of different groups. They work hand in glove so they have intelligence led. And on stop and search, let's be really clear, there is a place for stop and search, but it has to be based on intelligence, not on profiling. And the use of suspicionless stop and search is what builds up animosity and a distrust between communities and police, rather than building the trust that we so desperately need.

What we've done is already started detaining people. Airfields on standby, planes are booked. The date for the first flight is out on the 24th, I think it is, of July. We haven't given more details beyond that to not compromise the operational security, but there will be a regular rhythm of flights, not just one. And the important point here is this is about establishing a deterrent, because I believe that if people come here illegally, they shouldn't be able to stay. We need to have somewhere safe to remove them, and that's how we will stop the boats. And that's what Rwanda is about.
Which it has for millions of years.

We are going to train 3 million more apprentices. We're going to build 200,000 houses for people to buy. We're going to keep on with this reduction in unemployment that sees 2 million more people in work. But we won't do it if we go back to the debt, the welfare, the spending and the taxes.
Stephen, you weren't in agreement. You didn't want to put a levy on the oil and gas companies. That's not true, actually. We talked about a transition.
This is 20% of households you're talking about by the way, not the other 80%, but important.
that I disagree with Yes because

Well, I think again, you've got to pitch this in the overall totality of what you're trying to achieve with the local income tax. If you take pensioners, for example. Now we know many of those are being hit the hardest by council tax increases. Six million pensioners are going to be taken out of local taxation altogether. Now, you can say to me "here's one set of financial losers", and you're correct. I can say, there's one set of financial gainers and that is...
Because in both cases, different sets of circumstances, but in both cases the scheme, the timing, and what was in place we did not view as a party, were the correct schemes to put in place.

Yeah, I'm really happy to do that. Today, for example, we launched our policies for free personal care and for a higher minimum wage for care workers of £2 extra an hour to try to get more people into care, because that's desperately needed. And we've shown where the money will come from. We're saying that the tax cuts the Conservatives have given to the big banks should be reversed. That would raise £4.3 billion a year. Let me just take those figures though.

It was also about possibly the younger generation paying for other people's... Can I just... On the housing point, it's a very important point, because Rebecca, I'm sure, is, in keeping with lots and lots of young people, worried that you're not going to be able to get your feet on the first rung of the property ladder. The Liberal Democrats, we've got, I think, an idea which might help Rebecca, and it is this. At the moment, lots and lots of young people can't afford the deposit to get a mortgage on a property. So what we would do is we would introduce a rent-to-own scheme, where you wouldn't need to find the deposit to buy a house, but every time you rent, pay your rent at market rates, you'd build up a share of ownership in your home. So by renting, you'd become, over time, an owner in your home. I think that would be a great, great way, which we could introduce in the next Parliament, to give people like Rebecca that tangible belief that they can hope to live in a home which they can call their own.

Well, I wish we had the resources to do that at the moment. People are talking about the costing manifesto. We can't do anything. We've said in our manifesto, when resources allow, we want to do that. We did that before. The Conservatives said you can't do that. And we said we're going to do that. We must do that.

A hung Parliament provides Wales with a golden opportunity to end our decades-long funding disadvantage. Now, I asked you in the last debate whether or not you would be prepared to commit to making sure that Wales has parity with Scotland in terms of funding, which would enable us to practically end austerity by delivering an additional £1.2 billion into the Welsh economy. Instead of ploughing ahead with 2.2% cuts to the Welsh budget, will you now agree that Wales deserves parity with Scotland? And if not, can you justify why not?

£59 is not enough and we have got to do better as a society at giving people what we all want. Those that have done the right thing through their lives we should be giving you dignity and security in old age. Now I agree we need to uprate the pension in line with earnings rather than prices. The reason I feel I can give you that answer with total confidence that we really will deliver this is that we've made a tough decision to go with it. which is to say that from 2016 we're going to ask, starting with men, to retire a year later. That means we can fund this pledge because we all know we have an enormous budget deficit, there isn't any money left in the kitty, and so if we're going to make promises like that, and it's a big promise, an important promise, we've got to say how we're going to pay for it. Now I want to make sure that when we see people going into old age, that if you've worked hard, if you've saved, if you've put money aside, you're not punished. And that's why we say as well, it's not right that you should have to pay for your residential care and someone who didn't do the right thing gets the whole thing paid for free. That's not fair and we're going to change that.

Well, no one won it because there were no bold ideas. There was no honesty about how we're going to fund the changes that people actually need. And people are really, you know, democracy was the loser because there was no real promise of a brighter future.

What neither Labour nor the Conservatives are telling you is that their plans don't add up because they both want to leave the European Union and they both know, the government's analysis says and all the respected economic analysis says, that if we leave the European Union We will be poorer. There will be less money. So we won't be able to have these wish lists. And I have to say Peter you ask such a good question. You put your finger right on it. How can you guarantee it. Because both of them are trying to say that you can have this in the case of labor with somebody else paying for it and the case of the Conservatives saying that taxes won't have to go up. Do you know, if you're listening to that and you think in your heart of hearts you just know that's not true, then you need to look for parties that are going to set out where they will get the money from and we're saying very honestly we think the NHS needs more money and we're going to raise income tax by a penny in the pound in order to do so. You have to say where you're going to get the money from.

If you can cut waiting lists, I think that's a good thing to get more people treated more quickly. But I tell you what, I mean, basically what Rishi is saying about the NHS, you just cannot deliver it. The best way to protect the NHS from us begging the US for a trade deal is by not having to beg the US for a trade deal in the first place by remaining in the EU. And we have tens of thousands of doctors and nurses working in our hospitals as GPs, as carers, And we are already down 5,000 nurses from other EU countries in the last two years. What Rishi wants to do is then charge nurses from other EU countries for the privilege of coming to work in our NHS to use it. He wants to charge them 600 quid a year, a tax on nurses. How is that going to help our NHS get those vital nurses and doctors that we need to have to make sure that we look after people in our country?

Very directly, Christopher, I think we should be in Europe because we're a trading nation, we're part of Europe, we want to cooperate and work with our allies in Europe to get things done. But I do agree with you that we have let too many powers go from Westminster to Brussels. We've passed too much power over, and we should take some of those powers back. I want us to be in Europe, but not run by Europe. And you'll hear big differences between me and the other two parties. I don't want us to join the Euro, I want us to keep the pound as our currency. I don't want us to give up the British rebate, I want to make sure that we get value for money for what we put into Europe. I want to cut some of the bureaucracy, some of the rules, some of the regulations that I think drive business so mad. That's the agenda you'd get from a Conservative government in Europe. And to those who say somehow this would be isolation, I say nonsense. President Sarkozy of France, he stands up for France in Europe. Angela Merkel of Germany, she stands up for Germany in Europe. I would do exactly the same for Britain in Europe. Thank you. Nick Clegg.
I'll get it going between the two of you. Don't worry.

Look, I want to carry on. I'm 48 years old. I've got bags of energy. I believe in what the Liberal Democrats stand for, which is to strike the right balance between creating a strong economy but doing so fairly. I don't think that's represented on either the right or the left of British politics. But look, no, I have to stop you there.

Plaid Cymru want to deal with problems in the NHS so you are right staff are feeling pressure. That's why we want to train an extra 1,000 doctors and 5,000 nurses, half of which will work in our communities, over the next 10 years for the Welsh NHS. So we want to take a long-term approach. And on mental health, Plaid Cymru actually secured an additional £20 million for mental health support in Wales by being an effective opposition. And it's not enough to just identify problems. We have to be teaching children about mental health, because we all have mental health, and sometimes it will be better and sometimes it will be worse, just like with our physical health. So we need to teach people about mental health so that we can identify when maybe we are starting to feel not poorly. And then, of course, we have to resource support for it. In Wales, some people who receive treatment because of poor funding in mental health support have to travel to England to receive the support. And that's actually really problematic, because if you want to transition back home, And you want to do that slowly, one night at home, back in, two nights at home. It's very difficult to do that when you're traveling across miles and miles and hours and hours. So we want to continue investing in mental health support, continue investing in our NHS. And on making savings, we want to integrate social care into the NHS so that we have a social care and an NHS system which is working effectively together. Because firstly, without good social care, you know, people are going to not be able to recover and that's putting more strain on our GPs and on our hospitals, which is why it's crucial they're brought together. But also for patients' dignity and to be able to have good care, it's crucial that we have good care in the communities and that again, people can have that transition from hospital into good community care and back.

Yes, part of being a good leader is having a good strong team around you and I'm proud and delighted to be here representing the Conservative Party and the Prime Minister making that case. Let's face it, Jeremy only decided to come I think late this morning. I was rather hoping Diane Abbott might be here so I could debate with her as well. But the fact is the most important challenge that this government is going to have going forward is getting a good negotiation with the EU. It's going to dictate whether we have a strong economy, whether we can pay good wages to our public servants and support our public services. Theresa May has the support of her team. She has the support of whoever becomes her MPs. Jeremy Corbyn has had a no-confidence vote against him, which four out of five of his MPs wouldn't support him on. How can he go in to negotiate with 27 different countries with such a weak team and weak support behind him? Theresa May will be able to deliver for us. As Home Secretary, she delivered 35 different opt-outs. She knows how to negotiate and deliver for the European Union. She'll be the right leader for us.

Where are you moving them to? You don't even have a house for anyone else? either wherever they wish to go or to permanent sites. We're going to give more powers to create more permanent sites, but we don't think it's right that we have a planning system which is unequal and that some people have special rights within it and we're going to change that. Just to answer the point that the gentleman also said, absolutely right, we've also helped a quarter of a million young people onto the housing ladder through Help to Buy. That's continuing for the next few years. And we've also cut stamp duty, remember, so that unless you buy a very expensive property, £300,000, you won't pay any stamp duty as a first-time buyer.

The only evidence that we have are the opinion polls, which show a shift. But the only way to test it, ultimately, of course, is to have that confirmatory referendum. And I agree with you. Because politicians have failed to do their job, we have to take it back to the people, because it's only the people now that can end the impasse.

No, you made this point, and I'm not saying that they are, which is why we want to help that couple in other ways. If they are having a first child for example, the woman involved for that first child will receive a much higher level of maternity support, a hundred and seventy pounds under our proposals, than they do at the moment. If they have elderly parents that they're caring for there will be a much better - generous package of measures. And, if they've got children, whom they want to go to university in Cardiff or elsewhere, they will of course not be facing top fees and tuition fees. Now there's the package, and that's what we invite people to consider.

But the first stage to doing that is making sure that people can stay in their own homes. That's the urgent social needs payments. that should be available to all, so that they can stay in their own homes without worrying about finance. The next thing we've got to do is make sure if people have to go into old people's homes, then there is a limit on the costs they have to bear. Otherwise, they will lose their home and they have to choose between the care they need and the home that they own. So in the next Parliament, what we're proposing to do is put a two-year limit on payments, so that if you are, for example, suffering from Alzheimer's or other diseases, and you have to be in an old people's home for a longer period of time, can't be in your own home, we will suspend any payments after two years for personal and medical care.

Leon, I've changed Labour's approach on immigration because I think that the starting point has got to be that people's concerns are not based on prejudice. People's concerns are real and we have to address them. That's why we've said that if people come here they won't get benefits for at least the first two years because I think people should contribute before they claim. And it's why we've also said that we're going to deal with something which I don't think has been properly dealt with at all under David Cameron's government, which is migrant labour being abused, exploited, to drive down people's wages. You know, when I talk to people around the country, it's one of the biggest sources of concern and anxiety for them, because they say, my wages are being driven downwards. You've got migrant labour being brought in, paid less than the minimum wage, and nobody's doing anything about it. It's time to act. It's time to act on the rogue employers. It's time to act on the gangmasters. It's time to act on the rogue landlords. It's time to have fairness because I'm going to counter exploitation wherever I find it because we're going to protect the working people of this country.

The priority at the moment is making sure that we have an economic recovery. Making sure that we don't have higher unemployment. Unemployment in America is a lot higher than Britain. Unemployment in Europe is a lot higher. I want to keep people in their jobs. But take 6,000 million pounds out of the economy and you lose lots of jobs and you put businesses at risk. And David hasn't thought through his policy and neither has Nick if he's going to argue against us because it's the right thing to do.

My wife, I'm not a man of faith, but my wife is, Miriam is Catholic, my children are being brought up in her faith, so I have a little bit of an insight into the immense feelings of anguish in the Catholic community here and elsewhere. And I think many Catholics themselves feel really, you know, extremely, extremely torn apart by what's happened, and I think they do want to see the Catholic Church express greater openness and repentance. You can't keep a lid on sin, and of course you need to move with the times. I do welcome the Pope's visit, but I hope by the time he does visit there is a a greater recognition that there has been terrible, terrible suffering, there have been abusive relationships which have left immeasurable scars on individual people's lives and we need a process of openness and then healing. You can't undo the tragedies of the past but you can be open about them so people can start to move on.

Look, can I just put it like this? Over the years in the European Union and its European economic community, there have been a number of occasions where referendums have been held in countries. There was one in Ireland, I think France is an example as well. where they voted against what the EU was suggesting. And basically, the bureaucrats and the EU politicians turned around to those countries and said, you've got it wrong. Have another vote. We want you to come up with what we think is the right answer. But now we know how much it's going to cost. Sorry, you can come back in a minute. At that time, I think collectively, people here in the UK said, you know what? That's not the way to behave. If the people have given their choice, let's listen to the people and actually deliver on it. On the wrong information.
No. When we, when we ¿
We need to get it sorted and move on.

Look, I don't, by the way, think eight countries will leave. It's a possibility. Well, everything is a possibility. But I absolutely don't think it's going to happen. I'll tell you why. Because in a globalized world, where we have big borderless threats like climate change, human traffickers and crime which cross borders. We have these massive corporations that sort of go from one continent to the other. You've got capital flows going from one corner of the planet to the next. It makes sense. I think we become stronger when we do things together. I just think we can fight crime, we can fight climate change, we can regulate big global corporations better when we do it together. By the way, it's a very similar argument to why I so passionately believe in the country, the United Kingdom, that I love so much and I don't want to see it pulled apart. Because I think we are just, quite simply, stronger when we do things together rather than when we fall apart.

Listen, what they're going to take very seriously is that we would have been elected with a mandate, a mandate to negotiate a tariff-free trade access to the European market, that we will protect European nationals living here. What they will see is a government that is going to protect the protocols, environmental conditions, workers' rights that we achieved through Europe, and a government that wants a serious relationship with Europe but respects the result of our referendum in that we're leaving the European Union.

It's up to, for everyone, up to that amount. So that's a tax cut that's worth thousands of pounds of deposit that she otherwise wouldn't need to save for. And alongside that, we will also reintroduce a new version of Help to Buy, which allows her to buy a home with just a 5% deposit. Because when I talk to people like your daughter, what they say to me is, actually, I will be able to probably just about afford the mortgage. But what's tricky is the time it takes to save up for a deposit. And we saw with the previous version of Help to Buy that it makes a big difference, because it saves a young couple or a young person years in having to save up for that deposit, because the Help to Buy loan steps in to plug the gap and saves people an enormous amount of money. So Help to Buy, together with a stamp duty abolition for first-time buyers, essentially, will help young people like your daughter. Because I believe in a society where everyone can own their home. And if we're elected, we can cut taxes and build a home. So that's possible.

Trump's rise obviously concerns me hugely. It's not just his rhetoric, it's not just the use of populism, the use of hatred in his words, it's also his policies. When you look at what's actually happening to children in his detention. centres, I almost cannot cope with the thought of that. When it comes to our relationship with America, I just hope that after next year, when Donald Trump is gone, we can fix that and build something new. Because when I think about what we could do with that special relationship, with NATO's organisation, with its rapid reaction forces, and its logistics, and the kind of budget we're talking about putting behind Trident renewal, which is 200 billion pounds. The kind of peace project we could build with that kind of money and organisation, it's such a wasted opportunity.

OK. Misha, just to build on it, what the Chancellor did this morning was ask his opposite number, Keir Starmer's shadow Chancellor, to rule out raising all your taxes beyond what they've already announced in the first budget. and the Shadow Chancellor wasn't able to do that today. That's what the Chancellor did today, but you don't have to listen to me. Just go online, go to the Telegraph website, because we've just found a recording that they have put out there from the Deputy Chancellor from the Labour Party admitting that their plans will cost hundreds of billions of pounds.

Israel is losing the propaganda war. Look, the reality is, of course Israel has the right to defend itself. but it's got to make very careful judgments. And at the moment, when it makes mistakes, which it admits it's made, then it has to defend itself smartly and cleverly. But we have to get rid of Hamas.

The numbers have come down over the past month, as you acknowledge. But the way to solve this problem is to have a deterrent. And look, I'll tell you this. The Rwanda plan is a deterrent. You just have to listen to what the illegal migrants themselves are saying. One of them just said, most of us are still in France due to the fear we have about Rwanda. Another one said, I won't cross the channel until the Rwanda plan is destroyed. If Labour win, the people smugglers are going to need a bigger boat. Don't surrender our borders to the Labour Party.

Christine, the answer to your question is, what are they going to do about it? Neither of them will do anything. These people have lied to us repeatedly. And Labour... And Labour... And Labour... Repeatedly. I've actually always told the truth. Always said what I believe. And Labour, it's not even in your first six priorities for government. You haven't got the will to do anything about it. Nothing under these is going to change. And the vast majority, not on this panel, obviously, because they're not representative in that sense, the vast majority of Britons want net migration reduced significantly.
No, no, no, no. As far as it works, let's learn from it.
I, I've no idea what their

I would say to him that look at the US states that are actually moving in the right direction on climate change. It's good for the economy as well as good for the environment. Get your act together and get back into the Paris Agreement.

Well, we need the defence review so we can get everything that's required for the front line. I just want to go back to what I think Gordon didn't really tell you, which is after we deployed in Afghanistan at the end of 2005, For several years, I went each year, and each year, you didn't have to talk to many of our servicemen and women before they told you they simply didn't have enough helicopters. And to blame it on Taliban tactics, I think, frankly, is misleading. We didn't have enough helicopters. We needed more helicopters. We should have had helicopters. And if the government hadn't cut the helicopter program back in 2004, we probably would have had more helicopters.

Well Rebecca, we believe that education is a public good and therefore it should be paid for from general progressive taxation, far more progressive than we have now. Just look at the facts on this. Students on average now are leaving university with £44,000 worth of debt. 73% of them on current figures will never pay that off. They're going through 30 years of their life for many people from roughly their mid-20s to their mid-50s. Anytime you earn any sort of money at all, you're going to be paying a significant chunk of your income to a debt that you'll never repay. And it's not like this system is actually working. On current figures, again, 45p in the pound is never going to be repaid. So we in the Green Party not only want zero university tuition fees, we also want to pay off student loan company debts so people don't have that weight of debt. But of course, we also need changes in housing and jobs that you can build a life on, which is why we're calling for a minimum wage of £10 an hour by 2020.

Yes, we are. We are having an inquiry into Islamophobia and all types of prejudice in the Conservative Party. Of course we will do that. We will begin by the end of this year if we are lucky enough to be returned. Let me be clear that I am proud of the record of the Conservative Party in promoting women to... Which party has produced two female Prime Ministers? Ours. We have large numbers of women in the Cabinet today, I am delighted to say, and we have a very large and growing number, we have at least 35% of our candidates at this election are female. delighted by our record. Yes, of course, there is more that we can do. But under the Conservatives, the gender pay gap has shrunk, and we are doing everything we can to help women into the workforce, not least through childcare policies, some of which I announced today.

Could I come in here, just because, Laura, I think it feels to me like over the last 20 years in this country, we've had a lot of politicians pandering to rhetoric around migration. And we've allowed the debate to go way too far in one direction. And when I think we reached a low point in the referendum, and I'd like David to address this if possible, when Nigel Farage stood in front of that breaking point poster with pictures of Syrian refugees which had a remarkable similarity to Nazi propaganda, I think that was abominable. And I would like David to condemn that because we need to call that out when it happens.

So you're not going to give them the 35% pay rise that they're asking for, so how would you resolve it? Just standing there and saying, I'll resolve it, isn't an answer. That's not a plan. Prime Minister... How would you resolve it? Prime Minister, your record is... Well, no, no, how would you resolve it? You want to be Prime Minister. How would you resolve the strike? How would you resolve them?
Er, well I've no idea what you're thinking Jeremy.

I believe that we can negotiate the best type of Brexit deal, and the people can decide between that credible Brexit deal. But what I would say, Jo, what I would say with respect is, I think that the Liberal Democrats' policy of revoke if they got into government seeks to deepen divisions. It's not very liberal. It's not very democratic. And there are lots of Lib Dem supporters or previous Lib Dem supporters who are very upset about it.

I remember the question was about terrorism and extremism, and you notice that UKIP went straight for Muslims. We remember it wasn't a Muslim who shot Joe Cox, one of Jeremy's Labour MP colleagues. It was a British right-wing neo-Nazi. Who was it who gummed down kids in Norway? It was a Norwegian white racist neo-Nazi. There are all kinds of threats and we need to combat all kinds of threats.

Stuart asks would we participate. As I was your Prime Minister I would want to think very carefully what's in the national interest. What will make us safer here in the United Kingdom? And I think we need to end the division between sort of foreign policy and security policy and Home Office policy. Bring it all together and think about our national security. But I would also say this, if you look at future operations like that, we have to learn from the mistakes of the past. We've got to make sure that we plan properly. We've got to make sure we never send our troops into battle again without the proper equipment, without the proper helicopters. We have to think through not just what we're doing militarily, but actually is the aid there? Are we helping these people? Do we have a political strategy for how we're going to get out of that country once we've tried to make it safe with our allies? I think in the case of Afghanistan, far too many of those questions weren't answered. And even now, while I support what we're doing and I want it to work, I still worry we're not doing enough to get the political situation right in Afghanistan.
That is not something that...

All I'm saying is I don't think we should kid people into thinking that we can either justify or afford £100 billion over 25 years on a nuclear war system, a missile system, which was designed explicitly to flatten St Petersburg and Moscow at the press of a button. I think the world has moved on and I think you too need to move with it. We're not in the Cold War anymore and we shouldn't be spending billions of pounds of people's money, taxpayers' money, on a Cold War missile system when, as Nick said in his original question, we have people on the front line in Afghanistan without the right equipment, without the right protection. It's a question of priorities.
That is an illegal migration. And again, you have no plan to do it.
He did.
I think you can take it, Jeremy. No deal would be better than a bad deal. Yes, you've said that repeatedly. I'm not prepared to sign up to a bad deal for the UK. Now, I believe...
Thank you.

Months before this election campaign, within three weeks of taking office, the Prime Minister committed to toughening up sentences for violent criminals. He's been a long-standing believer that our system lets out people too early. And our view is that terrorists, those who have committed the most heinous crimes, should serve the full sentence that they are given. That is a reasonable position that the British people would rightly expect, and with a Conservative government, that's what we will deliver.

With us, you will get a deal which would be leave, but leave with a trade arrangement. Or you get remain, which will obviously carry on the current situation. But they would have the certainty of knowing what the trade relationship is, where the investment is going to come from, and where the market is for the goods that are made in this country. It's kind of important to know those things. Thank you very much, gentlemen. Very briefly.
- it shows that we... no, no, no. We, you (fluffs), we're going to cut taxes. The tax burden goes up when a, when an economy grows.

I'd like to answer your attack. I'd like to answer your attack. Of course, I have been to food banks. And the way to have people out of food banks, the way to have people not using food banks is to make sure that they can have a good job to go to.
OK, I'll just raise immigration, just for a change.

Fair votes for everybody. The truth is that Nick does support fundamental reform of the House of Commons and the House of Lords. But you haven't offered it. We are going to put it to a referendum next year and let the people decide. When politics breaks down in the way it did, and we had corrupt MPs, then we've got to have a new start, a referendum next year on a new House of Commons and a new House of Lords. That's the way forward. I'm sorry that the Conservatives reject these reforms.
But that's why I said it, because I think it was overloaded, it wasn't fully funded, and I think that, particularly now, in 2024, the base camp, the foundation, is economic stability.

Creativity, discipline, standards in schools, but we can't evade this question. If we're going to have the best education for our children, we do need the teachers and the teaching assistants. Now, if you cut money out of the education budget now, you'll be cutting the numbers of teachers and teaching assistants. Now, we say it's so important for our country that while we cut the deficit, we will maintain our investment in education per pupil. Now, the Conservatives cannot say this, and I think we need an answer this evening. Again, it's the risk, the risk to our health service, the risk in crime, if you have less, please. Now it's the risk to education.
