Well, I think there is real improvement in the health service. I'm really sorry if you've been waiting an unacceptable period of time. I don't know whether there are other people here who have used the health service recently, but I actually think when you go into our healthcare system, I'm not saying everyone, and I'm sorry that you've had a bad experience, but I think, on the whole, people get a very good service out of our National Health Service today. You, sir.

No, it's an absolute myth actually that the IPCC report was somehow advocating moving away from meat and milk, the kind of produce that we produce in a temperate, wet, largely wet climate like in Wales, actually animals fed on grassland make a positive contribution because grass is one of the most important stores of carbon that we have. If you lose that, if you move away from that, actually you move to scrub etc, then you actually actually lead to higher emissions as a result of that. The real message on food, right, is that we, all of us, as individuals, public authorities, schools, NHS, etc., we need to be buying more locally. We need to create local food systems producing the whole range, both of plant protein and animal protein, and stop importing it to the level we do. And farmers are an important part of the climate change that we want to create because they are custodians of the environment and we should be involving them as allies rather than decrying them as the enemies of...

Before we discuss Prince Andrew, I think we should discuss the victims that are there because of what Epstein was doing. And I think there are very, very serious questions that must be answered, and nobody should be above the law, but the primary position ought to be the proper treatment of those people who are victims of the most appalling behaviour by, apparently, Epstein and many others.

I've been crystal clear. I believe all our plans are compliant with our international obligations, but if I am forced to choose between securing our borders and our country's security or a foreign court, I'm going to choose our country's security every single time.

Because they've analysed everybody's thing, everybody's spending, everybody's child. Let me give you an example. Look, you know, I want to help pensioners in our country. We're going to have the triple lock on the basic state pension. That means pensions going up by 2.5% or prices or earnings. But we have said that the winter fuel allowance will not continue to go to pensioners with incomes above £43,000 a year. Because I don't think I can justify that in the times that we're in. But I think this point about the drivers of the welfare bill and what is actually making it go up is actually the key thing that is underlying it.

No. Let me explain why. I believe in a welfare system with responsibility. Responsibility means that if you can work, you should work. We're the only party at this election actually putting forward a proposal to say if you're a young person unemployed more than a year, we'll guarantee you a job working with the private sector, but if you don't take the job you'll lose benefits. Because I think that actually responsibility is the foundation of the welfare system. But let me just say one other thing to you, Amy, because it's important for keeping control of the welfare bill. Take the issue of housing benefit. You may not be obvious, but the housing benefit bill has gone up a lot in the last five years for those in work. Now, the reason for that is we have an economy based on low pay, and we're not building enough homes in our country. That's why we'll have an £8 minimum wage, and that's why we'll build homes again in Britain. You see, dealing with the welfare bill is about responsibility, but it's also about tackling the underlying factors that are driving the welfare bill up, like a low-pay economy that doesn't work for working people. And you know, this is all connected. Because our economy doesn't work for most people and works for the richest in our society, in my view, it means that the welfare bill is higher. It means that people are having a harder life and working all the hours that God sends. So we'll keep it under control.

The others don't agree about that, but I badly think we really, really need to stop this tax that could kill our recovery and kill jobs. I don't think it is. In many other parts of the world, it would seem to be a completely standard. I mean, Gordon Brown has talked about various bodies, but none of them actually involving the different parties. David Cameron says he'll try and make a hung parliament work, but spends all his time in the newspapers this week making those ludicrous claims about total meltdown, as if the world will end. The world won't end, and it'll be your choice. If you decide that no one here deserves an outright majority, then, of course, we'll just need to be open about it and talk to each other. We'll talk to each other in order to provide the good government, the sound government, that you deserve. Because you deserve a government where we put your interests first and don't allow everything, constantly, to be hijacked by short-term political point scoring.

Miles, what I think is really important is that you have the further education opportunities that you need, whether that's university or technical skills, I think either are perfectly, they're equivalent, they're really important. My dad was a toolmaker, so he went down the skills route. I was the first, as it happens in my family, to go to university. So we would ensure that those jobs are there for you for the future, that you have the opportunity to get on. will build the houses, so you don't have to be, what, 30-something, 35, 36 before you own a house under this government. But what I won't be doing, I won't be doing, is sending you on national service, some sort of teenage dad's army, which would be what the Prime Minister would say. Forget going on to university. He wants to send you off to do national service.

Can I try and move beyond the political point-scoring? We're not, as a nation, going to be able to balance the books, we're not going to be able to fill the black hole in our public finances unless we also do it with fairness right at the heart of everything we do. People aren't going to accept these difficult decisions unless we do it fairly. That's what I think. Accompanying the difficult job of actually filling the structural deficit, we also need to introduce a big tax switch so that people on ordinary incomes, low incomes, get a tax break - £700 back in their pockets - so they don't pay any income tax on the first £10,000 they've earned, by closing the loopholes at the top and using the money so that people feel that whilst difficult decisions are being made, at least the tax system is on their side. If you don't have fairness at the heart of everything we do, it's going to be very, very difficult to see us through these difficult decisions in the years to come.

I don't want to bore people with the figures, but it is important. Up until 1997, the highest number for net migration into the UK was about 77,000 in a year. Since 1997, since Labour came to power, it's never been lower than 140,000. Sometimes it's been 200,000. That's equivalent to 2 million across a decade. It's just too high. It can be brought down. We did it in the past. We should do it again.

Yes, yeah. The first thing I'd say is, we treat you with respect. And I say that because we have a Prime Minister who, 18 months or so ago, said, I'm going to get the waiting list down. And he said, hold me personally responsible. And now he hasn't got them down, because they've gone up and knocked down. He blames you. He blames the NHS staff. We will never do that, because I know how hard you work all of the time. in Covid in particular than the waiting list. But we do have to take the pressure off the waiting list. Now that is an ask of staff. It is an ask of staff. I'm not going to say otherwise because we will be working evenings and weekends to get those lists down. It will help because all of the hospitals I've been to say having that waiting list is a real drag on what else we can do. We will work, we'll pay the staff properly to do it. I'm convinced we can do it, by the way, because in St Thomas' in London and one of the Leeds hospitals I went to, I met the teams that are doing this already because they've decided to do that
Well it's not a question of having no idea.
No, we think that's an arbitrary sum.

What will happen on leaving the European Union is that we cease to be members of the single market and therefore free movement ends. What we're saying is that every EU national that is resident in this country must be given permanent rights of residence here and not blocked for it by this government. And secondly, that we would ask, as we've already done in opposition, that all European governments do the same for British nationals that are living there. And that we recognise the massive contribution made by people to this country have come from all over the world, as well as from Europe, to our health service, our education, our industries, our universities and our science.

Well he's absolutely right, that pensions should be linked to earnings and we'll do that in 2012 when we've got the resources to do so. We've also introduced the winter allowance for all pensioner households where someone is over sixty, and that's two hundred and four hundred pounds for people over 80. We're trying to do our best to create a new regime for pensioners where women particularly have a full state pension, which they haven't had in the past. To come to benefits, we're making it a condition for young people, they've got to take a job,we're making it a condition now for people who've been long term unemployed, that they've got to take a job. Yes we've got 2 and a half million more people now in work than there was in 1997, and yes single parents are working now when they used not to work, and yes we've got more young people in training and in education than we've had before. But yes also, we've got to go further, these are the measures of compulsion, a requirement to work and a responsibility to work.

What I'm not proud of is that we've had 14 years of the Tories and the crashing of the economy. But what I am realistic about is we've got to have a programme for government which is credible, that can grow our economy so we can invest in our green prosperity plan and so that we can eradicate poverty. And I know Labour can do it because we've done it before and we'll do it again.

Well, as I've just answered, I'm not going to give you a different answer from the one I've given Faisal. It's not about not knowing, Jeremy. It's about thinking what... Well, you don't know, do you? It's about thinking what the right approach is to get to that figure.

I'm fighting Labour's plans to tax our healthcare professionals on their pensions. That's what I'm fighting. Senior nurses, doctors... I don't think that's why anyone's leaving. ..are going to leave the NHS, and they're also going to be piling on additional pressures into our world. Penny, we haven't won the general election yet.

No, no, Abu Qatada and Hizb ut-Tahrir are who you work for as well, so people can Google that and have a look. But there's a clear choice. I'm investing more in our country's defence, up to 2.5% of GDP. OK. You are not doing that. So how can you tell people you will keep them safe when you're not willing to invest more in our country's security? Thank you.

Let's be real, we have over 100,000 vacancies in the NHS alone. That's not even counting social care, hospitality and other sectors. And I've said this before, but it hasn't become less true. If you meet a migrant in the NHS, they're more likely to be treating you than to be ahead of you in the queue. Now, some politicians, and unfortunately, the list of which politicians that is, is getting longer. like to scapegoat migrants for the problems with underfunding public services, when in reality, the cause of that is decades of decisions to underfund those public services by political parties. They could choose to make other decisions, they could choose to make bold decisions about how you pay for public services and how you raise those funds, and the Green Party is the only party in England being honest about that.

Well, I think in terms of the cuts to public services, that's had an impact. If you just take one small example, youth work. When you had a well-funded youth service, there were youth workers available to challenge the ideology of young people.
Fifteen minutes later,

And that's the problem. You've got these vast areas where it's not just how much it is, it's also there just isn't a dentist there, which is why... And how have we got to this state in 2024, where basic things like dentistry are a problem in our country? I mean, what on earth has happened to get us to this place? We've got to fix it. I look forward, on the first day we get into government, to starting on this job. I find all of that much more interesting than all the sort of talking about what you're going to do. I just want to get on with it.

We're talking about an increase in rents and people not being able to afford to pay rents. That's simply because we're not building enough houses. We're not building enough houses at the moment. You know, we have a problem. We're building 140... Hang on. We're building 140... Hang on. We're building 140... Maybe if we trained enough of our own people we wouldn't. Let me just finish. 140,000 houses per year that we're building at the moment. 80,000 of those are swallowed up by immigration. Alone!

You have your chance to speak. Relax. So we introduced the idea of actually both ways. The employee can decide to have three, four, five different contracts with different employers, and they can choose. There was a really interesting piece of work that McDonald's did the other day, and our fact checker hopefully will support me on this. So they offered all their zero-hours contract employees to go on a permanent contract. 80% of them said, no, we don't want to go on a permanent contract. We prefer the flexibility, whether they were students or for whatever reason, they wanted that flexibility. So we have to be careful before we sort of tar a hole. section of the workforce by saying, actually, you know what, these are all bad. Actually, people sometimes prefer that flexibility, especially if they're studying and want to work at certain hours, not all the time.

Well, I think on the issue of the European Union, I'm a passionate Remainer and lots of people have tried to say that I shouldn't be standing up for what I believe in and tried to say that, you know, it's wrong to argue for a people's vote. We've had the discussion tonight, but I've absolutely stuck to my guns and I will continue to do so to stand up for my principles and you can see that in action.
We want increased migration into Scotland because that's one of the ways you grow the economy.

No, no, - no Jeremy, excuse me. You can say what the backlog is of claims. The backlog of claims I think is down to round about ten thousand, that's down from sixty thousand that we inherited, right. In respect of removals, there were one in five asylum seekers who failed, were being removed. It's now half of them that are being removed.

No, we're committing to a balance plan which balances the books, not going much further. And I tried to set out what that balance plan is, but look, you and others will have to make a judgement about this, because I think we can balance the books without sacrificing our public services, without sacrificing tax credits. Jenny asked a question earlier, the very first question of this programme, where is Jenny who asked? who asked the question. Yeah. Look, Jenny, I'm afraid David Cameron, he might have sounded like he answered your question, but he didn't. But he didn't really give you a guarantee. I'm going to give you that guarantee tonight. I'm not going to cut your tax credits. I'm not going to cut child benefit or means test it. I think that would be the wrong thing to do for our country. the wrong thing to do for family financing. And I've got to say, after Mr Cameron's answers tonight, tax credits and child benefit are on the ballot paper of this election. Because millions of families risk losing thousands of pounds because of the cuts plan that he has. So yes, I do disagree with Mr Cameron. I've got a different plan.
Because of the record of this Prime Minister. So we've had...
Well, well one of the things that if you're serious about penal policy in this country, you've got to be serious about punishment for people who've offended. But you've also got to try...

Of course, the easy thing is to say how much we all love and depend and rely on the NHS. The difficult question, which is, I think, the one that you're addressing, is how do we protect the NHS, which we all rely on, maternity services, A&E departments, GP services and so on, when money is tight? And I think it's a little bit like the earlier discussion about equipment in the army. The priorities at the moment are all wrong. The last year, under this Government they have just employed 5,000 more managers in the NHS, yet the maternity ward in the NHS hospital where my third son was born just over a year ago is threatened with closure. This Government spent £12 billion on a computer system in the NHS which doesn't work and yet I was in Burnley the other day, I think Jacqueline was saying that you come from Burnley, As you know, Jacqui, they've closed the A&E department there. I think you have to now travel 25 miles to Blackburn. So what is going on? We're closing A&E departments and maternity wards and wasting money on computer systems and bureaucracy. I want to turn that on its head so we can protect the NHS that we all rely on.

Not at all, because we are putting forward the solution, and it's got to be a political solution. We can invest more in the police. The money is there if we have the political will to do it. We know that austerity was a lie. It didn't have to happen. People have suffered needlessly. And when you've got Extinction Rebellion protesters on the streets pointing the finger at politicians and saying, you have put our lives at risk. You are playing fast and loose with our future. You didn't take that step 12 years ago that you should have. This is our last means of resort. I'm very proud to put my body on the line and stand in solidarity with them.

Well, Stephen, the levels of migration are at record highs, 685,000. It's never been that high, save in the last year or two. The Prime Minister says it's too high. Well, who's in charge? Who's in charge? He's the most liberal Prime Minister we've ever had on immigration. We've never had numbers like this before. And he says that you can believe him on the boats. Nobody should be making that crossing across the Channel. 18 months ago, the Prime Minister made a promise. He said, just like the NHS promise, you're personally accountable for it, that he would stop the boats. This year alone, 10,000 people have crossed on boats. That's a record number. So again, he's made a promise, and he's completely failed to keep it.

Well, no, we've said we've put $10 billion in. We've put an additional $2 billion into the challenge around social care. And we're committing another $8 billion by 2022 into the NHS. But you need a strong economy to be able to do that.

Well, let me deal with that directly because I think we're gonna do something Which should have been done a long time ago probably by governments of both parties Which is to say that we'll have three-year tenancies not one-year tenancies in the private rented sector Because so many young people so many families are facing dreadful insecurity and within those three years We will cap the rents because I think it makes sense that people can have some stability when it comes to private rented housing and one other thing is we will also ban letting agents from charging tenants fees. At the moment, letting agents charge fees to tenants and to landlords is not fair, and lots and lots of people lose out in our country as a result.

If we're going to make a real difference, we've got to change the energy balance in our country. And we've got to remove this fixation about using oil, our addiction to oil, our dependence on oil. And that's why our energy plan talks about how we can move with nuclear and renewables and oil and gas. We want 15% renewables by 2020. We're the world's leading offshore wind power at the moment and we want to do more in every area for renewables. So the questions I have to ask the two other parties is this, why are you so against nuclear power because it is the means by which we can balance off our energy supply without having the dependence on oil? And David, why don't you have a renewables target as we do so that we can use wind power? Why are you so against the onshore wind power that people are trying to develop? You seem to support it in principle but in every constituency where it happens, you seem to be against it. Let's get real also about getting this energy balance right.

I have to say the answer is clearly no, we can't afford to. I do agree with you. We look at North Africa, we look at the Middle East, we look at huge tensions in neighbouring Russia. The world is in a very dangerous place, perhaps a more dangerous place than it's been at any point since the fall of the Warsaw Pact. It is astonishing that we saw the last government run down defence spending. Nobody thought that a Conservative Prime Minister would run the British Army down to the size of 82,000, and they still won't commit to 2%. In fact, from what I can see, almost nobody is prepared to commit to 2%. I think we should commit to 2%. I think we need to go back to 2010 manning levels and Trident, expensive though it is, I'm afraid is a necessary thing to have in the modern world. I would also like to see us spend just a little bit more money looking after our ex-servicemen and women who've come home from repeated conflicts and not been looked after properly.

We absolutely need to look at every single layer of our society to do this. Our Green New Deal looks at our homes, our industry, our energy, and very importantly, our agriculture, our food, and how we deal with our land. At the moment, we focus far too much of our land on pasture. We don't have enough woods. We need to plant an awful lot more trees. We need to rewild our landscape. What about red leech? And it means some of the landscapes that are currently being used for pasture land for meat need to be changed to being forestry, need to be changed to being absorbing carbon as well. And that's really important.

Is that really that important? Let me answer that directly, because I think it's important. I think the most important duty of any government, anyone who wants to be Prime Minister of this country, is to protect and defend our United Kingdom. And are we really happy to say that we'd give up our independent nuclear deterrent when we don't know what's going to happen with Iran, we can't be certain of the future in China, we don't know exactly what our world will look like? I say we should always have the ultimate protection of our independent nuclear deterrent.
This is from the government that cut the police force by such massive numbers, privatised our probation service and got rid of all of our PCSOs in many parts of the country.

But of course, freedom of movement benefits British people. We can move freely across the European Union. And people can come here too. And that leaves massively advantages to our business, to our tourism industry, to our health service. It's about understanding that talking about these issues should be done in the interest of the country, not demonising people who come from somewhere else for short-term political benefit.

Just respond to the point that this was in the documents, is the claim being made? I mean, people can see for themselves. And it's just a desperate conspiracy theory. We're absolutely crystal clear. The NHS will not be on the table. The price the NHS pays for drugs will not be on the table. We will walk away from any trade discussion where that's put on the table. We care deeply about supporting our NHS.

Health has been used as a political football by Labour and the Conservatives in order to try to score points off each other ahead of the general election and the people that suffer most when that happens are the patients and the staff in the Welsh NHS. Now there are problems in health in England and in Wales. And my view is that those problems are exacerbated by cuts to social services, but they're also exacerbated by privatisation. And it was the Labour Party that began the process of privatisation in the health service. They introduced PFI schemes and they introduced foundation hospitals as well. And my view is that the private sector has no role in a public health service.

Well, I think one thing I would like to add is this. Nadim, you're right in implying none of the political parties has spelt out all the details. Some of them are simply not possible to spell out now but clearly more work will need to be done. We've gone further than others but clearly more will need to be done. Something which I think would make a huge difference to us all, as we deal with these very difficult decisions about how to balance the books, is for once to get the politicians actually working together on this. I've suggested - I don't know whether David Cameron and Gordon Brown want to take up my invitation - is that, regardless of the outcome of the general election next week, wouldn't it be a good thing to get the Chancellor, and the vice-chancellors of all the parties together, with the governor of the Bank of England, with the head of the Financial Services Authority, simply to, sort of, be open and be straight with you about how big this black hole is and roughly how long it's going to take to deal with it, so that we're all at least speaking from the same script about how big this problem is. I think politicians for once putting the country before themselves would be a very good thing on this issue.

I think we've just heard, again, the phrase that we've heard a lot from Ed this evening. the working people of this country. I wonder, Ed, what your views are on the words of Rachel Reeves, your Shadow Work and Pension Secretary, who said, I don't want to represent people on benefits. I don't want to represent the unemployed. Well, personally, as the Green Party, we want to represent the most vulnerable in this country, the disabled people, the unemployed, the people who've really suffered under this and your previous governments, the people in poverty. Now, I think that people out there who think that we should be standing up for the disabled, for the Independent Living Fund, for getting rid of the horrendous work capability assessment that your previous government brought in, the dreadful ATOS, the privatisation, people who are against that, if you vote Green, you're expressing your distaste for that approach to life and saying we have to protect our most vulnerable.

What I've said is there should be a review of the individual prisoner before release is countenance. That, I think, is absolutely clear. And that's why I asked for a role for both the parole board as well as the probation service in this. Because, listen, what happened was unbelievably appalling. And, therefore, we have to keep people safe and secure. But you don't do that by under-investing in rehabilitation programmes as well for all prisoners.

Yeah, to the gentleman there, you're absolutely right. We do need to get out, and we do need to get out in a timely fashion. What I do worry about Theresa May's speech, some of it was good, but she seemed to indicate that we might have transition arrangements in many, many areas for an unlimited and unspecified number of years. And people voted to come out. We want our fishing waters back. We want to control our borders. We want to come out of the single market, and we want to do it in as quick a time as possible.

That's a very good question. Firstly, some context for that, as you're probably aware. Scotland, because of our disproportionately high drug use back in the 1980s, there's an ageing cohort of drug users that have other health conditions, which is one, it's not the only reason, but it's one of the reasons for that rising number of drugs deaths. It's a public health emergency. It's not acceptable. What we are doing about it, we're investing more money in drug treatment services. We've established a a task force made up of experts but also people with lived experience of drug use to look at the different, more innovative things that we need to do. Power over the classification of drugs is still held at Westminster. One of the things that Glasgow City Council is wanting to do, and we are seeking the power in order to do that, is to establish in Glasgow what's called a safe consumption room, so that we can try to reduce some of the harm related to drugs. But this is one of the most important issues that we're facing in Scotland, and I think there is a real... I know there is a real determination, but I think it exists across parties to do absolutely that.

The issues are of poverty in Britain. The issues are of differential levels of spending in inner city areas compared to other areas. And there are serious issues about the number of young black people, particularly young men, who are part of the criminal justice system, end up in youth custody or youth justice institutions, or those that end up in mental health institutions. I think we have to look very seriously at levels of racism within our society. If you like racism that's almost of an institutionalized nature, that has to be looked at. And one of the issues we put forward was consider how difficult it can be for somebody with an African name or a Muslim name to get a shortlist or get an interview for a job compared to others. And so one suggestion, it's a suggestion, it may become a policy, is that we should have blind applications for jobs where there's no name involved, there's merely a description of your skills and your ability and your qualifications, so that there can be a fair assessment of that person. Because we are, a multi-ethnic, multi-lingual, multi-cultural society. If it doesn't treat everybody equally and give everybody a decent chance, then we end up with poverty, we end up with discrimination and we end up with divisions. I want to see a country that comes together and is not divided and apart by poverty or by discrimination.

Well, Carly, thank you. I completely agree about the importance of tackling climate change. And I think in this regard, it's amazing that our children are probably setting an example for us politicians. I have two young girls, and they focus on the little things, turning off all the lights in our house, turning off the taps when they're brushing their teeth, and making sure everything goes in the right recycling bin. And as a government, we've tried to do some big things, some of it in coalition previously, but we've made the UK the first place to commit legally to net zero. We're leading the world in banning single-use plastics and being the first country to phase out the use of coal. And whilst I think all of us on this stage might have slightly different approaches to how we get there, I think we all share the ambition of having a cleaner, greener planet to leave behind for our children.

I voted against it at the time it came up, and we would set any expansion of any airport against tests on noise, on pollution, on sustainability, and on climate change targets. And if we are to reduce the amount of flying going on, then we do have to invest in extending and expanding our rail network, and particularly the rail network into Europe. I mean, I looked at the price of going to Berlin the other week. One airline was offering a £19 single ticket to go to Berlin. To go by train would have cost £240 and the rail infrastructure was totally invested in by the public. There has to be a much more efficient link-up. of the Eurostar rail service with the whole of the rest of Europe in order to reduce the number of short-haul flights that go to Europe and there has to be an expansion of our rail network all across the country if we are to get people to use trains rather than flights and also make the train fares cheaper so that it is actually a real offer, an option for everybody in this country.

Well the Liberal Democrats are the party of Paddy Ashdown so it won't surprise you to know that we take defence very seriously. So first of all we would maintain the nuclear deterrent that we have. An absolute priority for us is reversing the cuts to our troops of 10,000 which has happened under the Conservatives. We would increase spending on defence year on year in the next Parliament, with the ambition of reaching 2.5% by the end of the next Parliament. Having decent homes for our veterans and for our armed forces is incredibly important. I'm very proud of the fact that it was a Liberal Democrat amendment that tried to make sure that every single home met the decent home standard. It was only because this general election was called that that particular piece of legislation fell, but it's a priority for us in the next Parliament.

I think one additional point that I would make is if you look at where so much of the burglary, so much of the car crime is coming from, it is actually coming from people who are addicted to drugs. And I think we've got to be much faster at getting drug addicts off the streets and into treatment. and all too often that doesn't happen. And even when it does today, they actually just get put on a substitute drug. And we're not really dealing with the problem, which is to get these people to confront their problems and lead drug-free lives. I even went to a drug rehab recently in my own constituency and met a young man who told me that he committed a certain amount of crimes so he could get in front of a judge who could then get him a place in a residential rehab centre. We must be mad as a country not to get people into that residential rehab, get them to clean up their lives, so we cut the crime on our own streets.

You were told that austerity and inequality, bankers bonuses and tuition fees were inevitable. They were not. You all deserve better. Let's put principles and values first. That's why I got into politics. The Green Party is determined to deliver a fair economy that does not make the poor and disadvantaged pay for the errors and fraud of the bankers. We're committed to returning the NHS to its founding principles, no public money going into private profits. We know we must take real action on climate change, the biggest threat facing us all. Other parties trade in fear, fear of immigrants, demonising people on benefits. But to build a decent, humane society, we start with hope. Vote for change. Vote Green.

Well, over the last 14 years, if you leave out computer crime and online fraud, we have more than halved crime, 55%. And we have now a record number of police. Violent crime is down by 44%, but we have hotspots. and knife crime in particular, London is top of the list. We have put more police on the streets. We have met our manifesto commitment of 20,000 more police officers. We have record numbers. And where you have conservative police crime commissioners, like my area in Hampshire, you have local bodies on the beach.

I think the lady makes a very important point. Anyone who clearly can't stay at home, who has to live independently because of abuse or what have you, we have to make special provision for them and we would. But the situation today, where you can, age 18, leave school, sign on, get a flat, rather than work, or earn and learn at the same time. I don't think that's right. Look, other countries in Europe have almost abolished youth unemployment because they've taken this approach in, say, Germany or in Holland. And I think we should do the same thing. Look, we have created two million jobs in the last five years. Youth unemployment has come plummeting down. If we stick to the economic plan that's working, we can continue to get unemployment down and give young people what I want, which is the opportunity of an apprenticeship or a university place and the chance of a great career. Starting a life on benefits, frankly, is no life at all.

I've met some of the people who have rightly complained about the abuse that they were subject to when young, and it never leaves them. It's something that is with them always. And no matter what you can try to do to help, there is always this problem that they have to face up to every day, that they were abused, cruelly abused, by people in whom they placed their faith and trust. So the church has got to deal with these problems, and it's got to make sure that there is an open and clean confession about what has happened, and that we help those people who have been put into difficulty by this abuse. But you know, I welcome the Pope's visit to Britain and I want him to come to Britain for two reasons. One is the Catholic Church is a great part of our society and we should recognise it as such and I hope every British citizen wants to see this visit by the Pope take place. And secondly, we must break down the barriers of religion that exist in our world. The faiths must come together and recognise that they have common values and common interests They all believe that we should be good neighbours to each other. I'm from the Presbyterian religion, but I support the visit, but I not only support it, I want religious faith to work more closely together.

Well, I share the frustration of both our questions about the length of time it's taken to get Brexit done, and what we can do with the deal that we have is get it done by January 31st and come out, and then to get to the question about the advantages, we can do such things as improve our standards for animal welfare, ban the live export of animals, We can cut VAT on tampons. We can do things to create free ports around the country. There are all sorts of ways in which we can regulate differently and better. We can also, of course, control our immigration system for the first time in a long time. Now, what I haven't heard from the leader of the Labour Party is this deal that he thinks he can get in three months' time. Who is going to negotiate it? Because as far as I can see, everybody on the Labour frontbench is campaigning to remain, apart from Mr Corbyn, who is neutral on the matter, who is going to secure this deal? How can you get a deal, a new deal from Brussels for Brexit, if you don't actually believe in it? That's the mystery that I fell to.
You're saying people voted for 10% tariffs on cars?

I think this question of enthusiasm is really important that you put to us, because I'm not surprised that after 14 years of this, people feel that the hope's been beaten out of them, because the country is in such a state. They've had loads of promises made to them at the last election about what would happen, which haven't been delivered on. and beat the hope out of people. The very first question was about integrity in politics. And again, people haven't seen that integrity. You've had Partygate, you've had the breach of COVID rules, you've had the contracts for COVID, the instinct of some people to think the first thing in COVID I'm going to do is try to make money. Same with the gambling commission nonsense over the last few weeks. So this is an opportunity to restore that hope. I don't think we can do that by making grand promises of things that can't be delivered. I think it's the ordinary hope of working people. My family was working class. The ordinary hope of getting on yourself, getting on for your family, getting on for your community, and getting on for your country. It has to be rooted, if we're going to restore hope, in my view, in returning politics to service. Thank you. The sense that you come into politics to serve.
